---

## [A39] — Change Management Plan | Template

> **Usage note:** This is a blank template. Replace every `[FIELD: ...]` placeholder with project-specific content. Static rules, ADKAR definitions, legends, and checklist items are pre-filled and must not be replaced with field placeholders. Remove this usage note before publishing the completed plan.

---

## Section 1 — Header / Identification

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Project Name | [FIELD: full project name] |
| Sponsor | [FIELD: name and title of the project sponsor] |
| Change Lead | [FIELD: name and title of the individual leading organisational change management for this project] |
| Project Manager | [FIELD: name of the project manager] |
| Date | [FIELD: date this plan was created or last updated, YYYY-MM-DD] |
| Document Version | [FIELD: e.g., 1.0] |
| Status | [FIELD: select from Draft / Active / Under Review / Superseded] |
| Linked Artifacts | A07 — Benefits Realisation Plan; A06 — Project Management Plan; A10 — Communications Management Plan; A12 — Issue and Action Item Log; A14 — Resource Management Plan; A17 — Training Plan; A29 — Stakeholder Engagement Plan; A02 — Project Schedule |

---

## Section 2 — Change Impact Assessment

| Stakeholder Group | Current State | Future State | Change Description | Impact Level | Readiness Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: stakeholder group name, e.g., Finance Team] | [FIELD: description of how this group works today — processes, tools, behaviours] | [FIELD: description of how this group will need to work after the change] | [FIELD: concise description of the specific changes this group will experience] | [FIELD: select from Low / Medium / High / Critical] | [FIELD: description of the readiness level this group must reach before go-live] |
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: current state] | [FIELD: future state] | [FIELD: change description] | [FIELD: select from Low / Medium / High / Critical] | [FIELD: readiness required] |
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: current state] | [FIELD: future state] | [FIELD: change description] | [FIELD: select from Low / Medium / High / Critical] | [FIELD: readiness required] |
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: current state] | [FIELD: future state] | [FIELD: change description] | [FIELD: select from Low / Medium / High / Critical] | [FIELD: readiness required] |

**Impact Level Definitions:**
- **Low** — minimal disruption; normal communications and support sufficient
- **Medium** — moderate disruption; targeted engagement and training required
- **High** — significant disruption to roles, processes, or ways of working; active sponsorship and structured support required
- **Critical** — transformational or highly disruptive change; change management must be a primary delivery workstream

---

## Section 3 — Stakeholder Readiness Assessment

**ADKAR Model Definitions:**
- **Awareness** — stakeholder understands why the change is happening
- **Desire** — stakeholder is motivated to support and participate in the change
- **Knowledge** — stakeholder knows how to change
- **Ability** — stakeholder has demonstrated the skills and behaviours required
- **Reinforcement** — mechanisms are in place to sustain the change over time

**Readiness Level Definitions (per ADKAR element):**
- **Not Ready** — element is not yet addressed or evidence shows significant gap
- **Developing** — element is partially addressed; engagement or development activities are underway
- **Ready** — element is sufficiently addressed for this stakeholder group at this point in the project

| Group | Awareness | Desire | Knowledge | Ability | Reinforcement | Overall Readiness | Gap Summary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: select from Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: brief summary of the most significant gaps for this group] |
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: gap summary] |
| [FIELD: stakeholder group] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: Not Ready / Developing / Ready] | [FIELD: gap summary] |

---

## Section 4 — Change Adoption Strategy

| Narrative Element | Content |
|---|---|
| Overall Approach | [FIELD: describe the overarching change adoption strategy — the philosophy, sequencing logic, and primary mechanisms for building adoption across stakeholder groups] |
| Key Messages | [FIELD: list the two to five core messages that must be understood and accepted across all stakeholder groups — the "why this change, why now, what it means for you" narrative] |
| Critical Success Factors | [FIELD: list the conditions that must be true for the change management effort to succeed — e.g., visible and sustained sponsor engagement, adequate training resources, timely communications] |
| Assumptions | [FIELD: list the key assumptions underpinning this strategy — e.g., stakeholder groups will be receptive to structured engagement, executive sponsor will be available for key communications] |

---

## Section 5 — Resistance Management

| Resistance Category | Description | Likely Source Groups | Response Strategy | Owner | Monitoring Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: resistance category, e.g., Loss of Control] | [FIELD: description of the nature and drivers of this resistance type] | [FIELD: stakeholder groups from Section 2 most likely to exhibit this resistance] | [FIELD: specific strategy to address this resistance, e.g., co-design sessions, executive messaging, pilot involvement] | [FIELD: role or name accountable for implementing the response strategy] | [FIELD: how resistance in this category will be identified and tracked, e.g., pulse surveys, team leader feedback, engagement metrics] |
| [FIELD: resistance category, e.g., Workload Concern] | [FIELD: description] | [FIELD: likely source groups] | [FIELD: response strategy] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: monitoring method] |
| [FIELD: resistance category, e.g., Lack of Trust] | [FIELD: description] | [FIELD: likely source groups] | [FIELD: response strategy] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: monitoring method] |

---

## Section 6 — Change Champions Model

| Role | Name / Position | Responsibilities | Coverage Area | Engagement Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: champion role, e.g., Executive Sponsor Champion] | [FIELD: name or position of the individual in this role] | [FIELD: specific responsibilities of this champion, e.g., visible endorsement at team meetings, cascade key messages, surface resistance signals] | [FIELD: organisational area or stakeholder group this champion covers] | [FIELD: how often this champion engages on change activities, e.g., Weekly / Fortnightly / Monthly] |
| [FIELD: champion role, e.g., Team Change Champion] | [FIELD: name or position] | [FIELD: responsibilities] | [FIELD: coverage area] | [FIELD: engagement frequency] |
| [FIELD: champion role, e.g., Subject Matter Expert Champion] | [FIELD: name or position] | [FIELD: responsibilities] | [FIELD: coverage area] | [FIELD: engagement frequency] |

---

## Section 7 — Training and Capability-Building Plan

| Training ID | Target Group | Learning Need | Delivery Method | Content Owner | Scheduled Date | Completion Criteria |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: unique training identifier, e.g., TRN-001] | [FIELD: target stakeholder group] | [FIELD: specific learning need, linked to ADKAR Knowledge or Ability gaps from Section 3] | [FIELD: delivery method, e.g., Instructor-Led / e-Learning / Workshop / Job Aid / Coaching] | [FIELD: role or name responsible for content development and delivery] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD or planned date range] | [FIELD: how completion will be verified, e.g., Assessment pass rate ≥ 80%, attendance confirmed, practical demonstration] |
| [FIELD: unique training identifier] | [FIELD: target group] | [FIELD: learning need] | [FIELD: delivery method] | [FIELD: content owner] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: completion criteria] |
| [FIELD: unique training identifier] | [FIELD: target group] | [FIELD: learning need] | [FIELD: delivery method] | [FIELD: content owner] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: completion criteria] |
| [FIELD: unique training identifier] | [FIELD: target group] | [FIELD: learning need] | [FIELD: delivery method] | [FIELD: content owner] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: completion criteria] |

---

## Section 8 — Change Communications Approach

| Audience | Key Message | Channel | Frequency | Owner | Reference to A10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: audience, e.g., All Staff] | [FIELD: the key message to be communicated to this audience] | [FIELD: communication channel, e.g., All-staff email / Team briefing / Intranet article / Town hall] | [FIELD: e.g., Monthly / As milestones are reached / Weekly during cutover] | [FIELD: role or name responsible for delivering this communication] | [FIELD: reference to the corresponding communication item in A10 — Communications Management Plan, e.g., COMM-003] |
| [FIELD: audience] | [FIELD: key message] | [FIELD: channel] | [FIELD: frequency] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: A10 reference] |
| [FIELD: audience] | [FIELD: key message] | [FIELD: channel] | [FIELD: frequency] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: A10 reference] |
| [FIELD: audience] | [FIELD: key message] | [FIELD: channel] | [FIELD: frequency] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: A10 reference] |

---

## Section 9 — Adoption Metrics

| Metric | Baseline | Target | Measurement Method | Measurement Point | Responsible |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: metric name, e.g., Training Completion Rate] | [FIELD: current baseline value, e.g., 0% / Not measured] | [FIELD: target value to be achieved, e.g., 90% by go-live] | [FIELD: how the metric will be measured, e.g., Learning management system report / Survey / Usage analytics] | [FIELD: when this metric will be measured, e.g., 30 days post go-live / Monthly] | [FIELD: role or name accountable for measuring and reporting this metric] |
| [FIELD: metric name, e.g., Stakeholder Readiness Score] | [FIELD: baseline] | [FIELD: target] | [FIELD: measurement method] | [FIELD: measurement point] | [FIELD: responsible] |
| [FIELD: metric name, e.g., Resistance Incident Volume] | [FIELD: baseline] | [FIELD: target] | [FIELD: measurement method] | [FIELD: measurement point] | [FIELD: responsible] |
| [FIELD: metric name, e.g., Process Adoption Compliance] | [FIELD: baseline] | [FIELD: target] | [FIELD: measurement method] | [FIELD: measurement point] | [FIELD: responsible] |

---

## Section 10 — Transition and Sustainment Plan

| Phase | Key Activities | Owner | Success Criteria | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: phase name, e.g., Pre-Launch Preparation] | [FIELD: key activities in this phase, e.g., Complete training for all High/Critical impact groups; finalise champion network; confirm go-live communications schedule] | [FIELD: role or name accountable for this phase] | [FIELD: criteria that define successful completion of this phase] | [FIELD: target completion date, YYYY-MM-DD] |
| [FIELD: phase name, e.g., Go-Live and Hypercare] | [FIELD: key activities] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: success criteria] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] |
| [FIELD: phase name, e.g., Embedding and Reinforcement] | [FIELD: key activities] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: success criteria] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] |
| [FIELD: phase name, e.g., Benefits Realisation Check] | [FIELD: key activities] | [FIELD: owner] | [FIELD: success criteria] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] |

---

## Section 11 — Waste Test

The following checklist confirms that this plan adds clear value and avoids unnecessary overhead:

- [ ] The Change Impact Assessment in Section 2 is based on documented stakeholder analysis — not assumptions carried over from a prior project or template.
- [ ] ADKAR readiness levels in Section 3 are based on real engagement data (surveys, interviews, team leader input) — not optimistic estimates.
- [ ] The Change Adoption Strategy in Section 4 is specific to this project's context and stakeholder landscape — not a generic change management framework description.
- [ ] Resistance categories in Section 5 are grounded in early signals gathered from stakeholder engagement — not an exhaustive theoretical list.
- [ ] The Change Champions Model in Section 6 has named individuals who have confirmed their availability and role — not aspirational positions.
- [ ] Training items in Section 7 are linked to specific ADKAR gaps in Section 3 — no training is included that does not address a documented readiness gap.
- [ ] Communications in Section 8 are explicitly cross-referenced to A10 — the change management plan does not duplicate or contradict the Communications Management Plan.
- [ ] Adoption metrics in Section 9 are measurable and will be reported at defined intervals — not collected and filed without action.
- [ ] The Transition and Sustainment Plan in Section 10 extends beyond go-live to confirm that adoption is embedded before change management resources are withdrawn.

---

## Change Log

| Version | Date | Author / Event | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0.0 | 2026-05-31 | Phase B2 Batch C | Initial template |

---

# A39 — Change Management Plan

**Artifact ID:** A39
**Name:** Change Management Plan
**Version:** 1.0.0
**Status:** Active
**Source Type:** synthesis
**Governance Layer:** Project Governance
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.5 Stakeholders Performance Domain; §2.7 Delivery Performance Domain; Managing Change in Organizations Practice Guide

> **Source type note:** Organizational change management (OCM) is a recognized practice area in the PMI ecosystem via the *Managing Change in Organizations: A Practice Guide*. PMBOK 8 addresses the people-side of change through the Stakeholders and Delivery performance domains. This artifact synthesizes PMI change management guidance with project delivery needs. The artifact ID A39 is assigned per the repository's extended artifact catalog in Artifacts-V-1.0.5.

---

## 1. Purpose

The Change Management Plan describes how the people-side of project-driven organizational change will be managed. It addresses stakeholder readiness, adoption strategies, resistance management, communication related to organizational transition, training needs, and the measurement of change adoption.

This artifact is distinct from the Integrated Change Decision Log (A12), which handles project scope, schedule, and cost change requests. A39 addresses *organizational change* — the human and behavioral transition that occurs when a project delivers new capabilities, systems, processes, or ways of working that require people to change how they operate.

---

## 2. Scope

| In Scope | Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| Organizational change impact assessment | Project scope change requests and decisions (belong in A12) |
| Stakeholder readiness assessment and approach | Detailed project communications plan (belongs in A10) |
| Change adoption strategy and interventions | Risk register entries for change-related risks (belong in A19) |
| Resistance identification and management approach | Benefits realization tracking (belongs in A02) |
| Change training and capability-building plan | Technical implementation plans |
| Change champions and sponsor engagement model | HR policies or personnel actions |
| Change adoption metrics and measurement | Product acceptance criteria (belong in A08 and A09) |
| Transition and sustainment approach | Procurement activities |

---

## 3. Trigger Conditions

The Change Management Plan is created when:

- The project will deliver a significant organizational change (new system, process redesign, structural change, cultural shift) that requires people to adopt new behaviors.
- The business case or project charter identifies organizational readiness as a material success factor.
- The sponsor or governance authority requires a documented approach to managing the human side of transition.

The plan is updated when:

- Change adoption metrics reveal that readiness or adoption is below target.
- A new stakeholder group is identified that will be materially affected by the change.
- The project scope change alters the nature or timing of the organizational transition.
- Resistance patterns emerge that require an updated response strategy.

---

## 4. Required Sections / Fields

| Field | Description | Required |
|---|---|---|
| Change impact assessment | Which groups are affected, how significantly, and what they will need to do differently | Mandatory |
| Stakeholder readiness assessment | Current readiness level by affected group and target readiness required | Mandatory |
| Change adoption strategy | Overall approach to moving stakeholders from current to desired state | Mandatory |
| Resistance management approach | How resistance will be identified, understood, and addressed | Mandatory |
| Change champions model | Who will serve as change champions and how they will be engaged | Mandatory |
| Training and capability-building plan | What training or capability development is required and for whom | Mandatory |
| Change communications approach | How transition-specific communications will be delivered (references A10 for the full communications plan) | Mandatory |
| Adoption metrics | How change adoption will be measured and at what checkpoints | Mandatory |
| Transition and sustainment plan | How the organization will sustain the change after project delivery | Recommended |
| Sponsor engagement model | How the sponsor and senior leaders will visibly lead the change | Recommended |

---

## 5. PMBOK 8 Authority

| Source | Section | Purpose of Citation |
|---|---|---|
| PMBOK8 Guide | §2.5 Stakeholders Performance Domain | Grounds organizational change management in stakeholder engagement — readiness, resistance, and adoption are stakeholder outcomes |
| PMBOK8 Guide | §2.7 Delivery Performance Domain | Links change management to value delivery — a project delivers value only if the organization adopts and sustains the change |
| Managing Change | PMI Managing Change in Organizations: A Practice Guide | Primary OCM authority — provides the change management framework, readiness assessment, adoption strategy, and transition guidance |
| PMBOK8 Guide | Table 2-1 Manage Stakeholder Engagement | Change adoption activities are part of stakeholder engagement management |
| PMI Lexicon | Change Management Plan definition | Canonical PMI definition: a plan that provides direction for managing the change aspect of a project |

---

## 6. Used By (Skills That Consume This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Used |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-02 | Develop Project Management Plan | Change management plan is established as a subsidiary plan during integrated planning |
| SKL-04-06 | Manage Stakeholder Engagement | Change adoption activities and resistance management are executed as part of stakeholder engagement |
| SKL-05-07 | Monitor Stakeholder Engagement | Change adoption metrics feed into stakeholder monitoring |
| SKL-06-01 | Close Project or Phase | Transition and sustainment plan is activated as part of project closure |

---

## 7. Created By (Skills That Produce This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Produced |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-02 | Develop Project Management Plan | Produces the change management plan as a subsidiary planning artifact |

---

## 8. Linked Artifacts

| Artifact ID | Artifact Name | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| A07 | Stakeholder Register and Engagement Strategy | Stakeholder impact assessment and readiness levels in A39 are built on the stakeholder analysis in A07 |
| A06 | Tailoring Decision Record | Tailoring decisions determine whether a full change management plan is required or a lighter readiness section suffices |
| A10 | Communications Management Plan | A10 governs the overall communications approach; A39 contains change-specific communications strategy that A10 references |
| A12 | Change and Exception Decision Log | A12 handles project change requests; A39 handles organizational transition — they are complementary, not duplicative |
| A14 | Integrated Project Management Plan | Change Management Plan is a subsidiary plan within A14 |
| A17 | Integrated Performance Reporting Record | Change adoption metrics feed into integrated performance reporting |
| A29 | Change Readiness and Adoption Record | A39 is the plan; A29 is the living execution record tracking adoption status against the plan |
| A02 | Benefits and Value Measurement Record | Change adoption is a prerequisite for benefit realization — A39 adoption metrics link to A02 benefit triggers |

---

## 9. Validation Criteria

| Check | Validation Rule |
|---|---|
| Change impact assessment complete | At least two affected stakeholder groups are identified with impact description |
| Readiness baseline established | Current readiness level is assessed for at least the primary affected group |
| Adoption strategy defined | A documented approach to moving stakeholders from current to target state is present |
| Resistance management addressed | At least one resistance category is identified with a management response |
| Training needs identified | At minimum, a training needs statement is included even if detailed curriculum is TBD |
| Adoption metrics defined | At least one measurable adoption metric with a target and a measurement point is stated |
| Distinct from A12 | This plan does not contain project scope/schedule/cost change request records — those belong in A12 |
| Linked to A07 | Change impact groups trace to the stakeholder register in A07 |
| Waste test pass | Separate readiness assessment worksheet, resistance log, and change communications plan do not replicate the same OCM decisions without distinct decision value |

**Waste test:** Fail if a standalone organizational readiness workbook, a resistance tracking spreadsheet, and a change communications plan all contain the same impact assessment, adoption strategy, and metrics without each adding a distinct control or decision function.

---

## 10. Change Log

## [A01] — Business Case | Template

> **Usage note:** This is a blank template. Every `[FIELD: ...]` placeholder must be completed by the author before the artifact is submitted for governance review. Static text, checklist items, and structural elements must not be altered. Pre-filled fields (e.g., Linked Artifacts) are fixed by template design and must not be changed.

---

## Section 1 — Header / Identification

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Project / Initiative Name | [FIELD: full formal name of the project or initiative] |
| Initiative Reference | [FIELD: portfolio or programme reference code] |
| Sponsoring Organization | [FIELD: name of the organizational unit sponsoring the investment] |
| Investment Owner / Sponsor | [FIELD: name and title of the accountable executive sponsor] |
| Business Case Author | [FIELD: name and role of the person preparing this document] |
| Date Prepared | [FIELD: date in YYYY-MM-DD format] |
| Version | [FIELD: document version number, e.g., 0.1 Draft] |
| Status | [FIELD: select from Draft / Under Review / Approved / Superseded] |
| Authorization Stage | [FIELD: select from Pre-Authorization / Authorization Pending / Authorized / Rejected / Deferred] |
| Governance Body | [FIELD: name of the committee or board responsible for approval] |
| Linked Artifacts | A02 · A03 · A04 · A06 · A10 · A16 |

---

## Section 2 — Business Need and Strategic Alignment

**Business need / problem / opportunity statement**
[FIELD: describe the specific problem, unmet need, or strategic opportunity that this investment is intended to address; include relevant organizational context]

**Strategic objective(s) supported**
[FIELD: list the specific organizational strategic objectives or priorities that this investment advances]

**Alignment evidence**
[FIELD: cite the specific policy document, organizational strategy, portfolio plan, or regulatory instrument that this investment responds to — include document name, version, and section reference where available]

**Consequence of not acting (do-nothing impact)**
[FIELD: describe the measurable or qualitative impact on the organization if no action is taken, including financial, operational, reputational, or regulatory exposure]

**Urgency and timing drivers**
[FIELD: describe why this investment must be initiated now or within a specific timeframe — include any regulatory deadlines, market windows, contractual obligations, or dependency events]

---

## Section 3 — Options Analysis

| Option ID | Option Name | Description | Estimated Cost | Estimated Benefit | Key Risks | Feasibility Assessment | Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Option 1 | Do Nothing / Defer | Maintain the current state with no investment. Accept the consequences of inaction as described in Section 2. | [FIELD: cost of inaction or $0 investment] | [FIELD: nil or negative — describe benefit foregone] | [FIELD: risks of inaction] | [FIELD: feasibility of do-nothing posture] | [FIELD: Yes / No] |
| Option 2 | [FIELD: option name] | [FIELD: describe the second solution approach] | [FIELD: estimated total cost] | [FIELD: estimated total benefit] | [FIELD: key risks for this option] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low feasibility with brief rationale] | [FIELD: Yes / No] |
| Option 3 | [FIELD: option name] | [FIELD: describe the third solution approach] | [FIELD: estimated total cost] | [FIELD: estimated total benefit] | [FIELD: key risks for this option] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low feasibility with brief rationale] | [FIELD: Yes / No] |

**Options evaluation method used:** [FIELD: select from Cost-Benefit Analysis / Multi-Criteria Analysis / Feasibility Study / Expert Judgment]

---

## Section 4 — Recommended Option

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Recommended option name | [FIELD: state the name of the recommended option as defined in Section 3] |
| Rationale for recommendation | [FIELD: explain why this option was selected over the alternatives — reference evaluation criteria, financial analysis, risk profile, and strategic fit] |
| Key assumptions underpinning the recommendation | [FIELD: list all material assumptions that, if incorrect, would change the recommendation] |
| Critical dependencies | [FIELD: list all external conditions, decisions, resources, or events that must occur for the recommendation to succeed] |
| Key constraints | [FIELD: identify budget, time, resource, regulatory, or technology constraints applicable to the recommended option] |

---

## Section 5 — Expected Benefits and Success Criteria

| Benefit ID | Benefit Description | Type | Quantification | Measurement Method | Target Date | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| B01 | [FIELD: describe the first expected benefit] | [FIELD: select from Financial / Non-Financial / Strategic / Regulatory / Risk Reduction] | [FIELD: quantify in $ or %, or describe qualitatively if not financially material] | [FIELD: how this benefit will be measured] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD or period] | [FIELD: role or individual accountable] |
| B02 | [FIELD: describe the second expected benefit] | [FIELD: select from Financial / Non-Financial / Strategic / Regulatory / Risk Reduction] | [FIELD: quantify or describe qualitatively] | [FIELD: measurement method] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD or period] | [FIELD: role or individual accountable] |
| B03 | [FIELD: describe the third expected benefit] | [FIELD: select from Financial / Non-Financial / Strategic / Regulatory / Risk Reduction] | [FIELD: quantify or describe qualitatively] | [FIELD: measurement method] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD or period] | [FIELD: role or individual accountable] |

**Success criteria at investment level:** [FIELD: define the measurable conditions that must be met for this investment to be considered successful at the portfolio or programme governance level]

> **Note:** Full benefits tracking after authorization is recorded in A02.

---

## Section 6 — Financial Summary

**Cost Summary**

| Cost Category | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Investment | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] |
| Operating Costs | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] |
| Transition / Change Costs | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] |
| Contingency | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] |
| **Total Cost** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** |

**Benefit Summary**

| Benefit Category | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue / Savings / Avoided Costs | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] | [FIELD: $] |
| **Total Benefit** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** | **[FIELD: $]** |

**Financial Metrics**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Net Present Value (NPV) | [FIELD: $ value and discount rate used] |
| Return on Investment (ROI) | [FIELD: % and calculation basis] |
| Payback Period | [FIELD: months or years] |
| Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | [FIELD: % or Not Applicable] |
| Affordability conclusion | [FIELD: state whether the investment is within approved budget envelopes and funding is confirmed or contingent] |

---

## Section 7 — Major Risks and Assumptions

| Risk / Assumption ID | Description | Type | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation / Validation Approach |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RA01 | [FIELD: describe the first risk, assumption, constraint, or dependency] | [FIELD: select from Risk / Assumption / Constraint / Dependency] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: describe the mitigation strategy or validation action] |
| RA02 | [FIELD: describe the second risk, assumption, constraint, or dependency] | [FIELD: select from Risk / Assumption / Constraint / Dependency] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: describe the mitigation strategy or validation action] |
| RA03 | [FIELD: describe the third risk, assumption, constraint, or dependency] | [FIELD: select from Risk / Assumption / Constraint / Dependency] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: High / Medium / Low] | [FIELD: describe the mitigation strategy or validation action] |

> **Note:** Full risk entries are recorded in A19 after project authorization.

---

## Section 8 — Recommendation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Recommendation | [FIELD: select from Proceed / Defer / Reject / Proceed with conditions] |
| Conditions (if conditional) | [FIELD: state any conditions that must be satisfied before or during execution — leave blank if unconditional] |
| Requested authorization | [FIELD: describe the specific authority, funding, and mandate being requested from the governance body] |
| Next action following approval | [FIELD: describe the immediate next step — e.g., initiate project charter, assign project manager, allocate funds] |
| Revalidation triggers | [FIELD: list the events or threshold changes that would require this business case to be revalidated — e.g., cost variance >20%, strategic objective change, regulatory change] |

---

## Section 9 — Approvals and Sign-Off

| Role | Name | Signature / Electronic Approval Reference | Date | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [FIELD: governance role] | [FIELD: full name] | [FIELD: signature or approval reference] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: any conditions or notes] |
| [FIELD: governance role] | [FIELD: full name] | [FIELD: signature or approval reference] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: any conditions or notes] |
| [FIELD: governance role] | [FIELD: full name] | [FIELD: signature or approval reference] | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] | [FIELD: any conditions or notes] |

---

## Section 10 — Waste Test

The following checklist must be completed before submission to the governance body. Each item must be verified. Incomplete items must be resolved or documented as accepted exceptions.

- [ ] Strategic alignment is stated — not assumed
- [ ] At least two options evaluated, including do-nothing or defer
- [ ] Benefits are quantified where financially material — at minimum qualitatively described
- [ ] At least three investment-level risks or assumptions are listed
- [ ] A clear proceed/defer/reject recommendation is present
- [ ] Approval evidence reference is included or attached
- [ ] At least one revalidation trigger is identified
- [ ] Business case content is NOT duplicated inside the project charter
- [ ] Benefits tracking is deferred to A02 — not embedded here

---

## Section 11 — Change Log

| Version | Date | Changed By | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0.0 | 2026-05-31 | Phase B2 Batch D | Initial template |

---

# A01 — Business Case

**Artifact ID:** A01  
**Name:** Business Case  
**Version:** 1.0.0  
**Status:** Active  
**Source Type:** PMI-derived  
**Governance Layer:** Portfolio / Project Governance  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1.1 Governance Performance Domain; §4.5.1 Initiate Project or Phase  

---

## 1. Purpose

The Business Case documents the justification for undertaking a project or program. It establishes the value rationale, aligns the proposed investment with organizational strategy, summarizes options considered, and provides the authorization basis for the portfolio or sponsoring authority to approve or reject the investment.

The business case answers the fundamental governance question: *Why should this project be undertaken, and is it the right use of organizational resources?* It remains a living reference throughout the project lifecycle — it is revalidated at major decision points to confirm the investment thesis still holds.

---

## 2. Scope

| In Scope | Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| Strategic need or problem/opportunity statement | Detailed project management plan or execution approach |
| Alignment with organizational strategy and objectives | Detailed requirements specifications |
| Options considered and option selected | Detailed risk analysis or risk register |
| Expected benefits and value outcomes | Procurement strategy or contract terms |
| High-level financial analysis (costs, benefits, ROI, payback) | Stakeholder engagement strategy |
| Major risks and assumptions at investment level | Technical design or architecture |
| Recommendation to proceed, defer, or reject | Post-project benefits tracking (belongs in A02) |
| Success criteria at investment level | Tailoring decisions |
| Affordability summary and funding request | Detailed schedule or resource plan |

---

## 3. Trigger Conditions

The Business Case is created when:

- A business need, problem, or opportunity has been identified that may require organizational investment.
- A portfolio authority or sponsoring body requires a formal investment justification before authorizing work.
- An existing initiative is undergoing major revalidation due to changed strategy, market conditions, or performance.

The business case is updated or revalidated when:

- A major phase gate or decision point requires re-confirmation that the investment case still holds.
- Material changes to expected costs, benefits, or strategic alignment occur.
- A change request proposes a scope or investment change that alters the original value rationale.
- Project performance indicates that the expected benefits are no longer achievable.

---

## 4. Required Sections / Fields

Every Business Case must include the following fields:

| Field | Description | Required |
|---|---|---|
| Business need | The problem, opportunity, or regulatory driver that motivates the investment | Mandatory |
| Strategic alignment | How the proposed project aligns with organizational strategy and objectives | Mandatory |
| Options considered | At minimum two options, including the do-nothing option where applicable | Mandatory |
| Recommended option | The option recommended for approval with rationale | Mandatory |
| Expected benefits | Quantified or described benefits — financial and non-financial | Mandatory |
| Major risks and assumptions | Investment-level risks and key assumptions that could invalidate the case | Mandatory |
| Affordability summary | Indicative cost, funding source, and affordability conclusion | Mandatory |
| Success criteria | How the investment will be recognized as successful | Mandatory |
| Recommendation | Proceed, defer, or reject — with recommended next action | Mandatory |
| Owner | Named individual responsible for the business case | Mandatory |
| Constraints | Key constraints that bound the options or investment | Recommended |
| Revalidation triggers | Conditions that require the case to be reviewed before the next phase gate | Recommended |

**Format guidance:** Scale to context. A lightweight initiative may require only a one-page business case. Large or regulated investments may require a detailed structured document. The test is whether the business case answers the authorization question — not whether it meets a minimum page count.

---

## 5. PMBOK 8 Authority

| Source | Section | Purpose of Citation |
|---|---|---|
| PMBOK8 Guide | §2.1.1 Governance Performance Domain | Establishes value rationale, authorization context, and alignment with project initiation |
| PMBOK8 Guide | §4.5.1 Initiate Project or Phase | Confirms the business case as an input to the initiation process that leads to the Project Charter |
| PMBOK8 Standard | The Standard for Project Management §2.1 | Links value delivery thinking to the authorization basis for projects |
| PMO-Guide | PMO governance and project intake sections | Establishes the business case as the primary input to portfolio intake and prioritization |
| PMI Lexicon | Business Case definition | Canonical PMI definition: a documented economic feasibility study used to establish validity of the benefits of a selected component lacking sufficient definition and that is used as a basis for the authorization of further project management activities |

---

## 6. Used By (Skills That Consume This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Used |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-01 | Initiate the Project | Business case is the primary input to project charter development |
| SKL-03-02 | Develop Project Management Plan | Business case success criteria inform the planning baseline |
| SKL-05-01 | Monitor and Control Project Work | Business case benefits and success criteria are referenced during performance monitoring |
| SKL-06-01 | Close Project or Phase | Business case value expectations are validated during closure |
| SKL-06-02 | Final Performance Review | Business case provides the original investment benchmark for final performance assessment |

---

## 7. Created By (Skills That Produce This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Produced |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-01 | Initiate the Project | Reviews, validates, or produces the business case as part of project initiation activities |

---

## 8. Linked Artifacts

| Artifact ID | Artifact Name | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| A02 | Benefits and Value Measurement Record | Business case is the source document for benefits identification — A02 tracks benefits realization post-authorization |
| A03 | Feasibility and Viable Options Record | Feasibility analysis (A03) is a predecessor to or input into the business case options section |
| A04 | Project Charter | Business case is the primary input to charter development — the charter formally authorizes what the business case recommends |
| A10 | Intake and Prioritization Decision Record | Business case is the primary submission document for portfolio intake and prioritization |
| A06 | Tailoring Decision Record | Business case complexity and risk profile inform tailoring decisions |
| A16 | Financial Management and Cost Baseline Record | Business case affordability summary is the precursor to the detailed financial baseline |

---

## 9. Validation Criteria

A Business Case is considered valid when it meets all of the following:

| Check | Validation Rule |
|---|---|
| Strategic alignment stated | Explicit link to at least one organizational strategy objective or program goal |
| Options analysis present | At least two options evaluated, including a do-nothing or defer option |
| Benefits quantified or described | Expected benefits are stated with at minimum a qualitative description — financial benefits include a numeric estimate where applicable |
| Risks and assumptions identified | At least three investment-level risks or assumptions that could invalidate the case are listed |
| Recommendation present | A clear proceed, defer, or reject recommendation with rationale is included |
| Approval evidence | An approval signature, electronic approval reference, or portfolio decision record is attached or referenced |
| Revalidation plan | At minimum one revalidation trigger condition is identified |
| Waste test pass | Passes waste test — repeated value statements are not restated in multiple downstream templates without adding a new decision |

**Waste test:** Fail if the full business case content is duplicated inside the project charter. Fail if a benefits realization plan, strategic value tracker, and objective verification sheet all contain the same benefit statements without distinct decision value. Fail if the business case is never revalidated after material changes to scope, cost, or expected benefits.

---

## 10. Change Log

| Version | Date | Author | Change Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0.0 | 2026-05-30 | @fakhruldeen | Initial definition — Phase B1 Priority 2 |

**Template Version:** 1.0.0
**Status:** Template · Active
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1.2 Value Delivery System; Standard §3 Principles; Program-5E §4
**Owner:** Project Sponsor / Benefits Owner (post-project); PM (during project)

> **Usage Note:** Initiate this record during project authorization (SKL-02-01) using benefit statements from the Business Case (A01). Update the benefits baseline when planning is complete. Update benefits realization tracking at every reporting cycle. Complete the post-project handoff section (Section 6) before signing the Project Closure Record (A27). This record persists beyond project closure — hand it off to the named Benefits Owner.

---

## Section 4 — Value Measurement Plan

| Benefit ID | KPI | Data Source | Measurement Method | Measurement Frequency | Responsible Role | Reporting Destination |

| B-001 | [FIELD: KPI name] | [FIELD: data source] | [FIELD: how measured] | [FIELD: frequency] | [FIELD: role] | [FIELD: where results are reported] |

| [FIELD: B-NNN] | [FIELD: period] | [FIELD: actual] | [FIELD: target] | [FIELD: variance] | [FIELD: status] | [FIELD: notes] |

## Section 6 — Post-Project Benefits Handoff

Complete before project closure. A27 cannot be signed until this section is complete.

| Field | Value |

# A02 — Benefits and Value Measurement Record

**Artifact ID:** A02
**Artifact Name:** Benefits and Value Measurement Record
**Version:** 1.0.0
**Status:** Definition · Active
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1.2 · Standard §3 Principles · Program-5E · Portfolio-4E
**Source Type:** PMI-derived
**File Path:** `artifacts/initiating/A02-benefits-value-measurement-record.md`

---

## Definition

The Benefits and Value Measurement Record is the artifact that defines, tracks, and confirms the business benefits and value that the project is intended to deliver. It links project outputs (deliverables) to business outcomes (benefits) and organizational value, and it persists beyond project closure to support benefits realization tracking by the sponsoring organization.

**PMI Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1.2 — Value Delivery System; Standard §3 Principles (focus on value, stewardship); Program-5E §4 Benefits Management.

---

## Artifact Type

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Category** | Stakeholders |
| **Type** | Benefits realization and value record |
| **Lifecycle** | Initiated at project authorization (A01/A04) · Updated throughout project · Handed off at closure · Maintained post-project by benefits owner |
| **Baseline?** | Benefits baseline established at Planning; changes via change control |
| **Ownership** | Project Sponsor / Benefits Owner (post-project) · Project Manager (during project) |

---

## Purpose

- Define and quantify the business benefits the project is expected to deliver
- Link project deliverables to business outcomes and strategic objectives
- Establish measurable benefit indicators and baseline values
- Track benefit realization during and after project execution
- Provide the value justification referenced in A17 (§9) and A27 (§3)
- Support portfolio-level value reporting and benefits governance

---

## Sections

| Section | Content | Populated By |
|---|---|---|
| §1 Strategic Alignment | Link between project objectives and organizational strategy, portfolio/program context | SKL-02-01 |
| §2 Benefits Register | All identified benefits using schema below | SKL-02-01 · SKL-03-01 |
| §3 Value Measurement Plan | KPIs, measurement method, baseline values, measurement frequency, data source | SKL-03-01 |
| §4 Benefits Realization Tracking | Periodic measurement of benefit indicators against baseline | SKL-05-01 (ongoing) |
| §5 Post-Project Benefits Handoff | Benefits owner, handoff date, post-project tracking plan, governance authority | SKL-06-01 |

---

## Benefit Entry Schema (§2)

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Benefit ID** | Format: B-NNN (e.g., B-001) |
| **Benefit Name** | Short descriptive name |
| **Type** | Financial / Operational / Strategic / Compliance / Reputational |
| **Description** | Clear statement of the expected benefit |
| **Linked Deliverable(s)** | Project deliverable(s) that produce this benefit |
| **Benefit Owner** | Named individual accountable for realization |
| **KPI / Indicator** | Measurable indicator of benefit realization |
| **Baseline Value** | Current state value before project |
| **Target Value** | Expected value after project |
| **Measurement Method** | How and where data will be collected |
| **Realization Timeframe** | When benefit is expected to be realized (during/post-project) |
| **Status** | Not Started / In Progress / Partially Realized / Fully Realized |

---

## Inputs

| Input | Source |
|---|---|
| A01 — Business Case | Pre-authorization |
| A04 — Project Charter | SKL-02-01 |
| A05 — Context Register (strategic objectives) | SKL-01-01 |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register (benefit stakeholders) | SKL-02-02 |
| Portfolio/Program benefits framework | Organization |

---

## Outputs / Produces

| Output | Used By |
|---|---|
| Benefits baseline | A14 (PMP reference) · Governance |
| Benefits tracking data | A17 §9 (Performance Report) |
| Post-project handoff record | A27 §3 (Closure Record) · Benefits owner |

---

## Quality Criteria

- All benefits traceable to project deliverables and strategic objectives
- Every benefit has a named owner and measurable KPI
- Baseline values documented before project execution begins
- Realization timeframe specified (not all benefits realize at project close)
- Post-project handoff record completed before A27 is signed
- Benefits with post-project realization have a named benefits owner outside the project team

---

## Related Artifacts

| Artifact | Relationship |
|---|---|
| A01 — Business Case | Source of initial benefit assumptions |
| A04 — Project Charter | High-level benefit statements |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | Benefit stakeholders identified |
| A17 — Performance Report | Benefits tracking in §9 |
| A27 — Closure Record | Benefits handoff confirmed |
| A05 — Context Register | Strategic alignment reference |

---

## PMBOK8 Anchors

| Reference | Detail |
|---|---|
| PMBOK8 Guide §2.1.2 | Value Delivery System |
| PMBOK8 Standard §3 | Principle: Focus on Value · Stewardship |
| Program-5E §4 | Benefits Management in program context |
| Portfolio-4E §5 | Portfolio value and benefits governance |

---

| Version | Date | Change |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0.0 | 2026-05-30 | Initial definition — Patch G |
| 1.0.1 | 2026-06-01 | Migrated from artifacts/stakeholders/ to artifacts/initiating/ (P0-0I) |

---

*Owner: Project Sponsor / Benefits Owner · PM manages during project · Post-project: Benefits Owner*
*Retention: Per organizational records policy*

example_version: "1.0.0"

scenario: "Meridian CRM Modernization — Nexus Solutions Ltd. (fictional, anonymized)"
---

## [A04] — Project Charter | Example

> **Example notice:** This file contains a realistic but entirely fictional example of a completed Project Charter. All project names, organizations, people, and data are fabricated for illustration purposes only. This is not a real project record. Use this file as a reference when completing the blank template.

---

## Project Identification

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Project Name** | Meridian CRM Modernization |
| **Project Reference** | NSL-PRJ-2026-004 |
| **Programme / Portfolio** | Digital Transformation Programme 2026–2028 |
| **Sponsor** | Alex Rivera, VP Customer Experience |
| **Project Manager** | Jordan Patel, Senior Project Manager |
| **Charter Version** | 1.0 |
| **Charter Date** | 15 January 2026 |
| **Planned Start Date** | 19 January 2026 |
| **Planned End Date** | 30 June 2027 |
| **Total Duration** | 18 months |

---

## 1. Project Purpose and Justification

Nexus Solutions Ltd. currently operates a legacy on-premises CRM system (DataTrack v4.2) that was implemented in 2011. The system suffers from frequent performance outages, lacks mobile access capability, and cannot support the integration requirements of modern sales and customer support workflows. Licence renewal costs for DataTrack are projected to increase by 38% in 2027, and the vendor has announced end-of-life status for the platform effective December 2026.

The Meridian CRM Modernization project will replace DataTrack with a cloud-based CRM platform (Salesforce Sales Cloud and Service Cloud), migrating 15 years of customer and sales data, integrating the platform with the existing SAP ERP system and Microsoft Exchange email infrastructure, and training all 280 affected users. Successful delivery will reduce system downtime by an estimated 90%, improve sales cycle visibility for regional managers, enable remote access for field teams, and position the business to comply fully with evolving GDPR obligations under the EU Digital Markets Act.

---

## 2. Project Objectives

The following SMART objectives define success for this project:

1. **Platform Deployment:** Deploy Salesforce Sales Cloud and Service Cloud to production, with all core modules configured and user-accepted, by 31 March 2027 — as measured by sign-off on the System Acceptance Test report.
2. **Data Migration:** Migrate 100% of active customer records (estimated 47,000 records) and a minimum of 80% of historical interaction data from DataTrack to Salesforce, with a data quality score of ≥ 97%, by 28 February 2027.
3. **User Adoption:** Achieve a training completion rate of ≥ 95% across all 280 affected users and a 30-day post-go-live system utilisation rate of ≥ 80%, by 30 June 2027 — as measured by Salesforce adoption dashboards.
4. **Budget Compliance:** Complete the project within the approved budget of €2,400,000, with final cost variance not exceeding +5% of the approved baseline, by project close.

---

## 3. High-Level Scope

### 3.1 In Scope

- Selection, procurement, configuration, and deployment of Salesforce Sales Cloud and Service Cloud
- Migration of all active customer, account, opportunity, and case records from DataTrack v4.2
- Migration of historical interaction data (emails, call logs, notes) for the preceding 5 years
- Bi-directional integration with SAP ERP S/4HANA (customer master data, order sync)
- One-way integration with Microsoft Exchange (email-to-case routing)
- Role-based user provisioning and access management (SSO via Azure AD)
- GDPR compliance features: consent management, right-to-erasure workflow, data residency configuration (EU region)
- End-user training for Sales team (200 users) and Support team (80 users)
- Administrator and super-user training for IT Operations (8 users)
- Hypercare support period: 60 days post go-live
- Decommissioning of DataTrack v4.2 (data archival and server decommission)

### 3.2 Out of Scope

- Upgrade or replacement of SAP ERP S/4HANA (existing system remains in service)
- Replacement of Microsoft Exchange email platform
- Implementation of a Business Intelligence or advanced analytics layer (deferred to Phase 2)
- CRM integration with any third-party partner portals (deferred to Phase 2)
- Marketing automation module (Salesforce Marketing Cloud) — not included in this project
- Customer-facing self-service portal redesign

---

## 4. High-Level Deliverables

| # | Deliverable | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Salesforce Platform (Configured)** | Fully configured Sales Cloud and Service Cloud instance, including all workflows, page layouts, custom objects, and reports required for sales and support operations |
| 2 | **Migrated Data Set** | Validated, cleansed, and fully migrated dataset in Salesforce, including customer records, accounts, opportunities, cases, and historical interactions |
| 3 | **System Integrations** | Operational bi-directional SAP ERP integration and Exchange email-to-case integration, tested and approved by IT Operations |
| 4 | **Training Programme** | Completed role-based training programme including training materials, e-learning modules, classroom sessions, and training completion records for all 280 users |
| 5 | **GDPR Compliance Package** | Documented GDPR compliance configuration, privacy impact assessment, consent management workflows, and Data Processing Agreement with Salesforce |

---

## 5. High-Level Milestones

| # | Milestone | Target Date | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Project Kick-Off | 19 Jan 2026 | Project formally initiated; team mobilised; Salesforce contract signed |
| M2 | Requirements and Design Complete | 30 Apr 2026 | Salesforce solution design approved; data migration strategy approved; integration architecture signed off |
| M3 | Development and Configuration Complete | 31 Oct 2026 | All Salesforce configuration complete; integrations built and unit-tested; data cleansing complete |
| M4 | User Acceptance Testing Complete | 31 Jan 2027 | UAT sign-off received from Sales and Support team leads; all critical defects resolved |
| M5 | Go-Live (Phased) | 31 Mar 2027 | Sales Cloud live for Sales team (Phase 1); Service Cloud live for Support team (Phase 2, 30 Apr 2027) |
| M6 | Project Close | 30 Jun 2027 | Hypercare complete; DataTrack decommissioned; lessons learned published; project formally closed |

---

## 6. Budget Authority

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| **Total Approved Budget** | €2,400,000 |
| **Salesforce Licences (3-year, prepaid)** | €780,000 |
| **Implementation & Configuration (Salesforce SI partner)** | €620,000 |
| **Data Migration & Integration (contracted)** | €310,000 |
| **Internal Resource Costs (allocated)** | €240,000 |
| **Training Programme** | €120,000 |
| **Infrastructure & Security** | €95,000 |
| **Change Management & Communications** | €75,000 |
| **Project Management** | €110,000 |
| **Contingency Reserve (10%)** | €50,000 (management reserve held by Sponsor) |

The Project Manager (Jordan Patel) is authorised to approve expenditure up to €25,000 per item without additional sponsor approval. The Sponsor (Alex Rivera) must co-approve all commitments above €25,000.

---

## 7. Key Stakeholders

| Name / Role | Organisation | Responsibility | Interest Level | Influence Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alex Rivera, VP Customer Experience | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | Project Sponsor; champion; budget authority | High | High |
| Jordan Patel, Senior PM | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | Project delivery; day-to-day management | High | High |
| Miriam Osei, Head of Sales | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | Primary business owner; Sales team requirements | High | High |
| David Kowalski, Head of IT Operations | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | Technical authority; infrastructure and security | High | High |
| Fatima Al-Rashid, Legal & Privacy Officer | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | GDPR compliance approval; DPA sign-off | Medium | High |
| Clara Bates, Support Team Lead | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | Support team requirements; UAT coordination | High | Medium |
| TechBridge Solutions (SI Partner) | External | Salesforce configuration, integration, migration delivery | High | Medium |
| Salesforce Inc. | External (Vendor) | Platform, licences, tier-1 support | Medium | Medium |
| Sales Team (200 users) | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | End users; training participants | High | Low |
| Support Team (80 users) | Nexus Solutions Ltd. | End users; training participants | Medium | Low |

---

## 8. High-Level Risks

| # | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Initial Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R01 | **Data Migration Complexity:** Legacy DataTrack data contains significant duplication, incomplete records, and non-standard formatting; migration could exceed planned effort and timeline | High | High | Engage data quality specialist; conduct data profiling assessment by end of Phase 1; build contingency in migration timeline |
| R02 | **User Adoption Resistance:** Sales team accustomed to legacy workflows; low adoption post go-live could negate business case | Medium | High | Appoint Change Champions within Sales and Support teams; run early engagement workshops; include adoption KPIs in project success criteria |
| R03 | **GDPR Compliance Gap:** Salesforce platform configuration may not fully satisfy EU data residency and consent management obligations, creating regulatory risk | Medium | High | Engage Legal & Privacy Officer from project initiation; conduct Privacy Impact Assessment before go-live; include GDPR sign-off as a milestone gate |

---

## 9. Assumptions

1. Salesforce Inc. will maintain the contracted platform SLA of 99.9% uptime throughout the deployment and hypercare period.
2. Key business stakeholders (Head of Sales, Support Team Lead) will be available to participate in requirements workshops, UAT, and training for a minimum of 20% of their working time during peak project phases.
3. The existing SAP ERP S/4HANA system will not undergo significant version upgrades during the integration development period (March–October 2026).

---

## 10. Constraints

1. The project must achieve full go-live before DataTrack v4.2 reaches vendor end-of-life on 31 December 2026 (phased go-live with core functionality by 31 October 2026 is the hard gate).
2. The total project cost must not exceed the approved budget of €2,400,000; any scope additions requiring budget above this ceiling must be approved by the CFO.
3. All personal data processed or stored within the new CRM platform must reside within EU data centres, in compliance with GDPR Article 46 obligations.

---

## 11. Project Manager Authority

Jordan Patel is authorised as Project Manager with the following authority:

- **Scope:** Approve minor scope changes (estimated effort ≤ 5 person-days) without Change Control Board review; escalate all other scope changes.
- **Schedule:** Adjust task sequencing and sprint planning within approved phase milestones; adjustments to milestone dates require Sponsor approval.
- **Budget:** Approve individual expenditures up to €25,000; approve use of contingency reserve up to €15,000 per incident; escalate above those thresholds.
- **Resources:** Assign and re-assign project team members within the agreed team structure; escalate requests for additional headcount.
- **Vendor:** Manage day-to-day relationship with TechBridge Solutions; contract amendments require Sponsor and Procurement co-approval.

---

## 12. Sponsor Sign-Off

By signing below, the Sponsor formally authorises the Meridian CRM Modernization project, confirms the budget allocation, and designates Jordan Patel as Project Manager with the authorities specified in this Charter.

| Role | Name | Signature | Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Project Sponsor** | Alex Rivera, VP Customer Experience | *(signed)* | 15 January 2026 |
| **Project Manager** | Jordan Patel, Senior Project Manager | *(signed)* | 15 January 2026 |
| **IT Authority** | David Kowalski, Head of IT Operations | *(signed)* | 15 January 2026 |

---

# A04 — Project Charter Template

**Artifact ID:** A04  
**Artifact Name:** Project Charter  
**Template Version:** 1.0.0  
**Status:** Template · Active  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §4.5.1 — Initiate Project or Phase  
**Source Type:** PMI-derived  
**File Path:** `artifacts/initiating/A04-project-charter-template.md`

> **Usage note:** Complete all Mandatory fields before the project proceeds to planning. Fields marked Optional may be deferred only when the information is genuinely unavailable at chartering time and a plan exists to obtain it during planning. Do not use the charter as a substitute for the full project management plan.

---

## Section 1 — Project Identity

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Project Name** | [FIELD: Full name of the project] |
| **Project ID / Code** | [FIELD: Internal project identifier or reference code] |
| **Project Type** | [FIELD: e.g. Capital, Operational, Transformational, Regulatory, Product Development] |
| **Delivery Approach** | [FIELD: Predictive / Adaptive / Hybrid — preliminary; confirmed in A06] |
| **Charter Date** | [FIELD: YYYY-MM-DD] |
| **Charter Version** | [FIELD: e.g. 1.0] |
| **Prepared By** | [FIELD: Name and role] |

---

## Section 2 — Project Purpose and Justification

### 2.1 Business Need or Opportunity

[FIELD: Describe the problem, opportunity, or strategic need that this project addresses. State why the organization is undertaking this work now. Reference the Business Case (A01) if one exists.]

### 2.2 Strategic Alignment

[FIELD: State which organizational strategy, goal, portfolio objective, or regulatory requirement this project supports. Be specific — name the strategy or mandate.]

### 2.3 Expected Outcome

[FIELD: Describe what will be different, better, or resolved when the project is complete. Focus on the outcome for the organization, not just the deliverable produced.]

---

## Section 3 — High-Level Scope

### 3.1 In Scope

[FIELD: List the major deliverables, work areas, products, services, or results this project will produce. Use bullet points. Be specific enough to prevent scope creep without pre-empting detailed planning.]

- [Deliverable / work area 1]
- [Deliverable / work area 2]
- [Deliverable / work area 3]

### 3.2 Out of Scope

[FIELD: Explicitly list what this project will NOT deliver or address. This prevents scope assumptions.]

- [Exclusion 1]
- [Exclusion 2]

### 3.3 Key Assumptions

[FIELD: List the major assumptions that the project is based on. These will be refined and tracked in the Context Register (A05).]

| # | Assumption | Owner | Impact if False |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [Assumption statement] | [Owner] | [Impact] |
| 2 | [Assumption statement] | [Owner] | [Impact] |

### 3.4 Key Constraints

[FIELD: List the binding constraints on scope, schedule, budget, resources, or technical approach. These will be documented fully in A05.]

| # | Constraint | Type | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [Constraint statement] | Budget / Schedule / Scope / Resource / Regulatory / Technical | [Source] |
| 2 | [Constraint statement] | [Type] | [Source] |

---

## Section 4 — High-Level Milestone Intent

> **Note:** These are indicative milestones only. Detailed schedule planning occurs in A15.

| Milestone | Target Date (or Phase) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Project kickoff | [FIELD: date or phase] | |
| Planning complete / baseline approved | [FIELD: date or phase] | |
| [Key delivery milestone 1] | [FIELD: date or phase] | |
| [Key delivery milestone 2] | [FIELD: date or phase] | |
| Project closure | [FIELD: date or phase] | |

---

## Section 5 — Budget Boundary

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Approved Budget (indicative)** | [FIELD: Amount and currency, or range if not yet confirmed] |
| **Funding Source** | [FIELD: Budget line, cost centre, program fund, or other source] |
| **Budget Approval Authority** | [FIELD: Name or role of the person/body who approved this budget] |
| **Cost Tolerance** | [FIELD: Acceptable variance before escalation is required — e.g. ±10%] |

---

## Section 6 — Governance and Authority

### 6.1 Named Roles

| Role | Name | Contact |
|---|---|---|
| **Sponsor** | [FIELD: Full name] | [FIELD: email / contact] |
| **Project Manager** | [FIELD: Full name] | [FIELD: email / contact] |

| **PMO Representative** | [FIELD: Name or N/A] | [FIELD: contact] |

### 6.2 Project Manager Authority

[FIELD: Describe the level of authority delegated to the project manager. Specify what the PM can approve without escalation (e.g. budget reallocation up to X, scope changes within agreed boundaries, team assignments).]

### 6.3 Escalation Path

[FIELD: Describe the escalation chain — from project manager to sponsor to governance body. State what types of decisions require escalation.]

---

## Section 7 — Initial Success Criteria

# A04 — Project Charter

**Artifact ID:** A04  
**Name:** Project Charter  
**Version:** 1.0.0  
**Status:** Active  
**Source Type:** PMI-derived  
**Governance Layer:** Project Governance  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Guide §4.5.1 Initiate Project or Phase  

---

## 1. Purpose

The Project Charter formally authorizes the existence of the project and grants the project manager the authority to apply organizational resources to project activities. It establishes the high-level boundaries of the work, names the sponsor and project manager, and provides the foundational authorization record that all subsequent planning and execution artifacts depend upon.

Without a formally approved charter, the project has no recognized authority to consume organizational resources, make decisions, or obligate the organization to any commitments.

---

## 2. Scope

| In Scope | Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| Formal project authorization and authority grant | Detailed project management plan or subsidiary plans |
| Named project manager with defined authority level | Full requirements specifications or acceptance criteria detail |
| High-level scope boundary statement | Detailed schedule, cost estimate, or resource breakdown |
| High-level milestone intent (not a baseline schedule) | Procurement strategy or contract terms |
| Initial budget boundary (not a cost baseline) | Stakeholder engagement strategy (belongs in A07) |
| Named sponsor and initiating authority | Risk register or detailed risk analysis |
| Initial success criteria at governance level | Technical design or architecture decisions |
| High-level assumptions and constraints | Tailoring decisions (belongs in A06) |

---

## 3. Trigger Conditions

The Project Charter is created when:

- A business case (A01) or equivalent authorization document has been approved by the relevant portfolio or program authority.
- The organization has made a decision to proceed with a defined scope of project work.
- A project manager needs to be formally designated with named authority.
- A feasibility or options analysis (A03) has recommended proceeding.

The charter is updated when:

- The project scope boundary changes materially and requires re-authorization at the governance level.
- Sponsor or project manager changes require re-establishment of authority.
- Phase transitions (in multi-phase projects) require re-authorization for the next phase.

---

## 4. Required Sections / Fields

Every Project Charter must include the following fields:

| Field | Description | Required |
|---|---|---|
| Project purpose | One to three sentence statement of why the project exists | Mandatory |
| High-level scope boundary | What is in scope and what is explicitly out of scope | Mandatory |
| High-level milestone intent | Key milestones or phase gate targets — not a baseline schedule | Mandatory |
| Budget boundary | Approved funding ceiling or indicative budget range | Mandatory |
| Named sponsor | Full name and role of the project sponsor | Mandatory |
| Named project manager | Full name, role, and delegated authority level | Mandatory |
| Initial success criteria | How success will be recognized at the governance level | Mandatory |
| Approval authority | Who approved the charter and on what date | Mandatory |
| Assumptions | High-level assumptions that affect authorization | Mandatory |
| Constraints | High-level constraints that bound the project | Mandatory |
| Stakeholder references | Reference to identified key stakeholders (detail in A07) | Optional |
| Related documents | Business case ID, feasibility record ID, portfolio decision reference | Recommended |

**Format guidance:** The charter must be concise. It is an authorization record, not a planning document. Repeating the full business case narrative or embedding detailed plans inside the charter violates the waste test (see Section 9).

---

## 5. PMBOK 8 Authority

| Source | Section | Purpose of Citation |
|---|---|---|
| PMBOK8 Guide | §4.5.1 Initiate Project or Phase | Establishes the charter as the output of the Initiate Project or Phase process and defines its role as the formal authorization mechanism |
| PMBOK8 Guide | Governance Performance Domain §2.1 | Grounds the charter in governance thinking — authorization, oversight, and decision rights |
| PMBOK8 Guide | Stakeholders Performance Domain §2.5 | Confirms that charter creation begins the stakeholder identification process |
| Process-Groups | Initiating Process Group | Confirms the charter as the primary output of the Initiating Process Group |
| PMI Lexicon | Project Charter definition | Canonical PMI definition: a document issued by the project initiating sponsor that formally authorizes the existence of a project and provides the project manager with the authority to apply organizational resources to project activities |

---

## 6. Used By (Skills That Consume This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Used |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-01 | Initiate the Project | Primary output — skill produces the charter |
| SKL-02-02 | Identify Stakeholders | Uses charter as primary input to identify stakeholders |
| SKL-03-01 | Plan Project Integration | Uses charter as planning baseline input |
| SKL-03-02 | Develop Project Management Plan | Charter is the foundational authorization reference for all subsidiary plans |
| SKL-04-01 | Direct and Manage Project Work | References charter to confirm scope authority during execution |
| SKL-05-01 | Monitor and Control Project Work | References charter success criteria for performance monitoring |
| SKL-06-01 | Close Project or Phase | Validates closure against charter-defined success criteria |
| SKL-07-01 | Select and Tailor Lifecycle Approach | Uses charter scope and constraints to inform lifecycle selection |

---

## 7. Created By (Skills That Produce This Artifact)

| Skill ID | Skill Name | How Produced |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-01 | Initiate the Project | Primary producing skill — drafts and obtains sponsor approval for the charter |

---

## 8. Linked Artifacts

| Artifact ID | Artifact Name | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| A01 | Business Case | Charter input — the business case justifies and precedes the charter |
| A03 | Feasibility and Viable Options Record | Charter input — the recommended option from feasibility analysis informs charter scope |
| A05 | Context Register (EEF/OPA) | Charter output feeds the context register — EEFs and OPAs identified during chartering are captured in A05 |
| A06 | Tailoring Decision Record | Charter feeds tailoring — complexity and constraints identified in the charter drive tailoring decisions in A06 |
| A07 | Stakeholder Register and Engagement Strategy | Charter initiates stakeholder identification — A07 is produced using the charter as input |
| A08 | Scope and Requirements Decision Record | Charter scope boundary is the authorization basis for detailed scope definition in A08 |
| A14 | Integrated Project Management Plan | Charter is the root authorization reference for the integrated plan |

---

## 9. Validation Criteria

A Project Charter is considered valid when it meets all of the following:

| Check | Validation Rule |
|---|---|
| Authorization completeness | Named sponsor and named project manager with explicit authority level are both present |
| Scope boundary present | Both in-scope and out-of-scope items are defined |
| Budget boundary present | At minimum, an indicative budget ceiling or funding approval reference is stated |
| Success criteria defined | At least one measurable or observable success criterion is stated |
| Approval evidence | An approval signature, electronic approval reference, or governing body decision record is attached or referenced |
| No planning duplication | The charter does not contain a full project management plan, detailed schedule, or full requirements list |
| No business case repetition | The charter references the business case rather than restating its full content |
| Waste test pass | Creating or maintaining this artifact takes less than 30 minutes per cycle and drives an authorization decision |

**Waste test:** Fail if the charter repeats the complete business case and full plan set. Fail if a charter is created but no approval authority reviews or signs it. Fail if the charter is updated for minor scope adjustments that do not require re-authorization at governance level.

---

## 10. Change Log

| Version | Date | Author | Change Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0.0 | 2026-05-30 | @fakhruldeen | Initial definition — Phase B1 Priority 1 |
