# REF-02 — Introduction to Disciplined Agile Delivery (2nd ed.)
**Shortcode:** DAD-Intro  
**Publisher:** Disciplined Agile, a PMI company  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Disciplined Agile Toolkit and Hybrid Delivery Scaffolding  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Ambler, S. W., & Lines, M. (n.d.). *Introduction to Disciplined Agile Delivery: A Small Agile Team's Journey from Scrum to Disciplined DevOps* (2nd ed.). Disciplined Agile, a PMI company. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/disciplined-agile/books/introduction-to-disciplined-agile-delivery

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
Disciplined Agile Delivery (DAD) extends PMBOK 8's lifecycle options by providing a full process goal framework — a "choose your WoW (Way of Working)" toolkit that allows project teams to tailor their delivery process across 24 process goals. Where PMBOK 8 acknowledges tailoring as a principle (P1 — Holistic View) and a practice (Tailoring Appendix), DAD operationalizes it at the team level with decision-tree based process selection.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | DAD-Intro Contribution |
|---|---|
| Development Approach & Life Cycle (§2.3) | Defines Scrum-based, Lean, Continuous Delivery, and Exploratory lifecycles |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Light-touch governance and team self-organization within enterprise guardrails |
| P7 — Tailor Based on Context | DAD's "Choose Your WoW" is the definitive tailoring implementation model |
| P6 — Build an Empowered Culture | Team autonomy with enterprise alignment: the DAD core premise |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A06 — Tailoring Decision Record | DAD process goal framework informs tailoring choices in A06 |
| A09 — Delivery Approach and Team Operating Agreement | DAD lifecycle options map directly to A09 delivery approach section |
| SKL-03-02 — Select Development Approach | DAD lifecycle selection criteria supplement APG for enterprise contexts |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- Use DAD-Intro when the project operates within a larger DA-enabled enterprise or needs to align with an existing DA toolkit rollout.
- DAD's phase model (Inception → Construction → Transition) maps to PMBOK 8 lifecycle phases and provides a useful bridge between predictive and adaptive thinking.

---
*Authority: Disciplined Agile Delivery (2nd ed.) · PMBOK8 §1.3, §2.3*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-03 — AI Essentials for Project Professionals
**Shortcode:** AI-Essentials  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2024  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — AI Literacy and PM+AI Integration  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2024). *AI Essentials for Project Professionals*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/ai-essentials-for-project-professionals

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
PMBOK 8 Appendix X5 introduces AI as an emerging force in project management but does not detail AI competency development for practitioners. AI Essentials bridges this gap by defining AI literacy levels for PMs, covering prompt engineering basics, AI-assisted planning tools, risk assessment for AI use, and ethical guardrails. It is foundational reading for practitioners before using AI agents or co-pilots in a PMO context.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | AI-Essentials Contribution |
|---|---|
| Appendix X5 — AI and Emerging Technology | Core operational detail for the AI appendix |
| P3 — Embed Quality | AI output quality assessment, hallucination risk, and validation practices |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | AI accountability: human oversight, decision traceability, model governance |
| P5 — Integrate Sustainability | AI energy consumption and ethical AI considerations |
| Risk Performance Domain (§2.7) | AI-specific risk categories: bias, data drift, model failure, privacy exposure |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A37 — AI, Data, and Privacy Control Record | AI-Essentials provides the risk taxonomy that populates A37 |
| SKL-08-01 — AI Use Case Assessment | AI-Essentials is the primary literacy reference for this skill |
| SKL-08-02 — AI Decision Support and Oversight | AI-Essentials governance and accountability model applies here |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- Before deploying an AI co-pilot on a live project, practitioners should complete the AI Essentials competency framework as a readiness check.
- Use AI-Essentials Chapter 4 (Risk and Ethics) when populating A37.

---
*Authority: PMI AI Essentials for Project Professionals (2024) · PMBOK8 Appendix X5*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-04 — Guide to Leading and Managing AI Projects
**Shortcode:** AI-Projects  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2025  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — AI Project Governance and Delivery  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2025). *Guide to Leading and Managing AI Projects*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/leading-and-managing-ai-projects-digital-guide

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide addresses the full project lifecycle specifically for AI/ML projects — from problem framing and data strategy through model governance, deployment, and ongoing monitoring. It provides PM-specific guidance on managing data science teams, handling model versioning, coordinating with MLOps pipelines, and governing AI output quality. It fills the gap between general PM standards and the practical realities of AI delivery.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | AI-Projects Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | AI project governance model: steering, model review boards, data ethics committees |
| Risk Performance Domain (§2.7) | AI-specific risk register categories: bias, drift, adversarial inputs, compliance exposure |
| Scope Performance Domain (§2.2) | AI scope challenges: defining done for probabilistic models, data scope |
| P3 — Embed Quality | Model evaluation metrics, test set governance, performance threshold management |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Human-in-the-loop accountability structures for automated decisions |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A37 — AI, Data, and Privacy Control Record | AI-Projects provides the governance structure that A37 must document |
| A19 — Risk Management Record | AI-specific risk categories from this guide extend the standard risk register |
| SKL-08-01 — AI Use Case Assessment | Core reference for assessing AI project viability and governance fit |
| SKL-08-02 — AI Decision Support and Oversight | This guide defines the oversight model that this skill implements |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- Use this guide as the primary reference when the project deliverable includes an AI/ML model, algorithm, or automated decision system.
- Chapter 5 (Governance and Accountability) is essential before completing A37.

---

*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-05 — Leading AI Transformation
**Shortcode:** AI-Transformation  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2025  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Organizational AI Strategy and PMO Transformation  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2025). *Leading AI Transformation: Organizational Strategies for Project Professionals*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/leading-ai-transformation-organizational-strategies-for-project-professionals

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide operates at the organizational and portfolio level — addressing how PMOs and portfolio leaders should respond to AI-driven transformation. Where PMBOK 8 focuses on individual project delivery, this guide addresses change management for AI adoption, building AI-ready cultures, restructuring PMO services for an AI-augmented workforce, and measuring transformation value. It is the companion for PMO Directors overseeing enterprise-scale AI integration.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | AI-Transformation Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | PMO governance evolution for AI-augmented delivery |
| P5 — Integrate Sustainability | Long-term AI workforce impacts: reskilling, displacement, ethical technology adoption |
| P6 — Build an Empowered Culture | Building an AI-ready culture: trust, transparency, human-AI collaboration norms |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Executive accountability for AI transformation outcomes |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A39 — PMO Configuration and Authority Record | Informs PMO restructuring decisions in AI transformation contexts |
| A36 — PMO Value, Maturity, and Improvement Record | AI capability maturity dimensions extend the PMO improvement model |
| SKL-07-01 — Configure PMO | AI transformation readiness is a PMO configuration consideration |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- Primary reference for PMO Directors planning AI enablement across a portfolio of projects.
- Use the transformation readiness model from this guide when assessing organizational AI maturity before deploying PMOSkills AI agent features.

---
*Authority: PMI Leading AI Transformation (2025) · PMBOK8 §2.1, Appendix X5*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-06 — Enabling Project Management Transformation with GenAI
**Shortcode:** GenAI-Transformation  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2025  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — GenAI Adoption in PM Practice  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2025). *Enabling Project Management Transformation with GenAI: The Need for Organizational Support*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/learning/thought-leadership/genai-the-need-for-organizational-support

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This thought leadership guide addresses the specific challenges and organizational conditions required for GenAI tools to deliver value in project management contexts. It identifies the gap between individual GenAI tool adoption and organizational transformation, arguing that sustained PM value from GenAI requires structural enablement: governance, training, policy, and cultural readiness. It is directly relevant to PMOSkills' own mission as a machine-readable AI-ready PMO framework.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | GenAI-Transformation Contribution |
|---|---|
| Appendix X5 — AI and Emerging Technology | Operational context for GenAI adoption patterns and failure modes |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Organizational accountability for GenAI tool governance and oversight |
| P6 — Build an Empowered Culture | Cultural readiness as a prerequisite for GenAI effectiveness |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | GenAI policy, acceptable use, and output validation governance needs |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A37 — AI, Data, and Privacy Control Record | GenAI-specific risks and governance requirements extend A37 |
| A06 — Tailoring Decision Record | GenAI tool adoption decisions should be documented in the tailoring record |
| SKL-08-02 — AI Decision Support and Oversight | GenAI output oversight model applies directly to this skill |

# REF-07 — Business Analysis for Practitioners (2nd ed.)
**Shortcode:** BAP-2E  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2024  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Business Analysis, Requirements, and Needs Assessment  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2024). *Business Analysis for Practitioners: A Practice Guide* (2nd ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/business-analysis-second-edition

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
PMBOK 8 defines requirements elicitation and analysis as a scope process (§2.2.2.2) but does not provide technique-level depth. BAP-2E is the complete practical guide for business analysis within project contexts — covering needs assessment, stakeholder analysis, elicitation techniques (workshops, interviews, observation, prototyping), requirements documentation, prioritization methods (MoSCoW, Kano), and traceability. It is the go-to reference for any project with significant requirements complexity.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | BAP-2E Contribution |
|---|---|
| Scope Performance Domain — Elicit and Analyze Requirements (§2.2.2.2) | Full technique repertoire for requirements elicitation |
| Stakeholders Performance Domain (§2.5) | Stakeholder analysis and communication planning for BA work |
| P2 — Focus on Value | Value-driven requirements prioritization and scope trade-off methods |
| P3 — Embed Quality | Requirements quality criteria: testability, completeness, consistency, traceability |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

# REF-08 — The PMI Guide to Business Analysis
**Shortcode:** PMI-BA  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2017  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Business Analysis Knowledge Framework  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2017). *The PMI Guide to Business Analysis*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/business-analysis

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The PMI Guide to Business Analysis is a comprehensive knowledge framework that defines BA as a discipline with its own knowledge areas, tasks, and competencies. It aligns BA work with project and portfolio management, clarifying the PM/BA role interface — a persistent source of confusion in practice. For PMOSkills, it is the foundational reference establishing when and how BA tasks integrate with project delivery governance.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | PMI-BA Contribution |
|---|---|
| Scope Performance Domain (§2.2) | BA knowledge areas map to scope planning, elicitation, and validation |
| Stakeholders Performance Domain (§2.5) | BA stakeholder engagement model complements PM stakeholder processes |
| P2 — Focus on Value | Needs assessment and value realization are BA core competencies |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A08 — Scope and Requirements Decision Record | PMI-BA knowledge framework informs the requirements governance structure of A08 |
| SKL-03-03 — Plan Scope Management | BA planning tasks align with scope management planning in this skill |

# REF-09 — Requirements Management: A Practice Guide
**Shortcode:** Requirements  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2016  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Requirements Lifecycle Management  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2016). *Requirements Management: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/requirements-management

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide focuses on the full requirements management lifecycle — from elicitation through validation, change, and traceability — across predictive, adaptive, and hybrid delivery models. It provides the traceability matrix methodology, change control procedures for requirements, and acceptance criteria governance that are essential for complex product or systems delivery. It is more operationally detailed than BAP-2E on the governance and control side.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Requirements Contribution |
|---|---|
| Scope Performance Domain (§2.2) | Requirements traceability, change control, and scope validation |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Requirements baseline governance and approval authority |
| P3 — Embed Quality | Acceptance criteria definition, testability standards, requirements quality gates |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A08 — Scope and Requirements Decision Record | Requirements management lifecycle is documented and governed through A08 |
| SKL-05-03 — Validate Scope | Requirements traceability and acceptance criteria validation is the core of this skill |
| SKL-03-04 — Elicit and Analyze Requirements | Change control procedures for requirements apply during planning |

# REF-10 — Implementing Organizational Project Management
**Shortcode:** OPM-Implementation  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2014  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — OPM Maturity and PMO Implementation  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2014). *Implementing Organizational Project Management: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/shop/p-/book/implementing-organizational-project-management-a-practice-guide/00101500501

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide provides the implementation roadmap for standing up OPM capability in an organization — covering maturity assessment, gap analysis, implementation planning, and change management for OPM adoption. Where the OPM Standard (REF-12) defines what OPM is, this guide explains how to build it. It is the primary reference for PMO Directors undertaking a PMO setup or capability expansion initiative.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | OPM-Implementation Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | OPM governance structure, PMO authority model, and portfolio/program/project integration |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | OPM accountability structure: clear authority lines from project to enterprise |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | OPM maturity as a system-level capability — not project-by-project improvement |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A39 — PMO Configuration and Authority Record | OPM-Implementation's PMO design model informs the PMO configuration documented in A39 |
| A36 — PMO Value, Maturity, and Improvement Record | OPM maturity assessment methods provide the baseline for A36 |
| SKL-07-01 — Configure PMO | This skill's PMO design steps are grounded in OPM-Implementation |

# REF-11 — Governance of Portfolios, Programs, and Projects
**Shortcode:** GPPP  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2016  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Multi-tier Governance Framework  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2016). *Governance of Portfolios, Programs, and Projects: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/governance

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
GPPP defines the governance framework across all three delivery tiers — portfolio, program, and project — providing the structural logic for how governance decisions flow between layers. PMBOK 8 defines governance at the project level; GPPP extends this upward into portfolio governance boards, steering committees, and enterprise governance bodies. It is the primary reference for establishing the Governance and Decision Authority Record (A35) and for designing multi-project governance structures.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | GPPP Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Multi-tier governance structure, forums, authority, and accountability |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Governance as the mechanism for making leadership accountability explicit |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | Portfolio-level view: all projects within a governance system, not in isolation |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A35 — Governance and Decision Authority Record | GPPP is the primary authority reference for A35 governance structure |
| A39 — PMO Configuration and Authority Record | GPPP defines the PMO's role within a broader governance hierarchy |
| AUTHORITY-ROUTING.md | GPPP T1–T4 governance layer model informs the decision threshold model |
| SKL-02-04 — Establish Governance Structure | GPPP governance design principles are applied in this skill |

---

# REF-12 — The Standard for Organizational Project Management (OPM)
**Shortcode:** OPM-Standard  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2018  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — OPM Principles and Maturity Model  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2018). *The Standard for Organizational Project Management (OPM)*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/organizational-project-management

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The OPM Standard defines OPM as the integration of portfolio, program, and project management with enabling organizational practices. It establishes the OPM maturity model — the framework for assessing how well an organization consistently applies PM practices to achieve strategic objectives. It is the definitional standard that underpins all PMO maturity and capability improvement work in PMOSkills.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | OPM-Standard Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | OPM governance integration across portfolio, program, and project tiers |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | OPM accountability chain from executive to delivery |
| P2 — Focus on Value | OPM value delivery model: strategic alignment through portfolio selection |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A36 — PMO Value, Maturity, and Improvement Record | OPM maturity dimensions are the assessment framework for A36 |
| A39 — PMO Configuration and Authority Record | OPM Standard defines PMO types and authorities referenced in A39 |
| SKL-07-01 — Configure PMO | OPM Standard is the definitional reference for PMO capability design |

---

# REF-13 — Project Management Offices: A Practice Guide
**Shortcode:** PMO-Guide  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2025  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — PMO Design, Services, and Value  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2025). *Project Management Offices: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/pmo

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The PMO Practice Guide (2025) is the definitive current PMI guidance on PMO design, service catalog development, value measurement, and governance integration. It defines PMO types (Supportive, Controlling, Directive, and Value Delivery), PMO service categories, and the PMO value maturity model. It is the primary reference for all Pack 07 PMO Skills in PMOSkills and for the PMO-layer governance artifacts (A11, A23, A36, A39).

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | PMO-Guide Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | PMO as a governance enabler — service model, authority, and oversight functions |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | PMO accountability: service ownership, value reporting, governance forum facilitation |
| P2 — Focus on Value | PMO value proposition, benefit measurement, and value delivery office model |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | PMO as the integrative layer across multiple projects and portfolios |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A11 — PMO Service Engagement Record | PMO-Guide service catalog model defines the service types documented in A11 |
| A23 — PMO Improvement Backlog | PMO-Guide improvement cycle and backlog management practices apply to A23 |
| A36 — PMO Value, Maturity, and Improvement Record | PMO value maturity model from this guide is the basis for A36 |
| A39 — PMO Configuration and Authority Record | PMO type taxonomy and authority model from this guide define A39 |
| SKL-07-01 to SKL-07-04 — All Pack 07 PMO Skills | This guide is the primary authority for all PMO skills |

Project Management Institute. (2017). *The Standard for Portfolio Management* (4th ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/for-portfolio-management

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The Portfolio Management Standard defines the processes and governance model for managing a portfolio of projects and programs to achieve strategic objectives. It provides the intake, prioritization, balancing, and performance monitoring framework that sits above the individual project level. For PMOSkills, it is the authority reference for portfolio governance artifacts (A10, A22) and for understanding how project governance fits within a portfolio governance system.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Portfolio-4E Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Portfolio governance: intake, authorization, performance monitoring, strategic alignment |
| P2 — Focus on Value | Portfolio value optimization: balancing investment across the portfolio for maximum strategic return |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | Portfolio as a system: interdependencies, shared resources, and strategic coherence |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A10 — Intake and Prioritization Decision Record | Portfolio-4E intake and selection processes are the authority for A10 |
| A22 — Portfolio Interdependency Record | Portfolio interdependency management model from this standard informs A22 |

# REF-15 — The Standard for Program Management (5th ed.)
**Shortcode:** Program-5E  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2024  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Program Governance and Benefits Realization  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2024). *The Standard for Program Management* (5th ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/program-management-fifth-edition

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The Program Management Standard defines program governance, benefits management, stakeholder engagement, and organizational change management at the program level — the tier between individual projects and portfolio management. It is particularly relevant for PMOSkills practitioners managing interconnected projects whose combined outcomes deliver benefits that no single project could achieve alone. It defines the benefits realization governance model that informs A02 and A24.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Program-5E Contribution |
|---|---|
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Program governance: program board, benefits ownership, and component authorization |
| P2 — Focus on Value | Benefits management as the primary program management value delivery mechanism |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Program manager accountability for benefits realization across component projects |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A02 — Benefits and Value Measurement Record | Program-5E benefits register and realization tracking model informs A02 |
| A24 — Closure and Benefits Transition Record | Program-level benefits transition governance applies to project closure |
| SKL-06-03 — Transition Benefits to Business Owner | Program benefits realization methodology informs this skill |

# REF-16 — The Standard for Earned Value Management
**Shortcode:** EVM  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2020  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Integrated Performance Measurement  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2020). *The Standard for Earned Value Management*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/earned-value-management

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
EVM Standard provides the full technical specification for integrated cost-schedule performance measurement using Earned Value Analysis. While PMBOK 8 references EVM metrics (CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC) as monitoring and control tools, this standard defines the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) construction, work authorization procedures, and the formal Integrated Baseline Review (IBR) process. It is essential for any project requiring formal EVM compliance (defense, government, large infrastructure).

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | EVM Contribution |
|---|---|
| Finance Performance Domain (§2.4) | Cost baseline construction, cost variance analysis, EAC forecasting |
| Schedule Performance Domain (§2.3) | Schedule performance index (SPI), time-phased baseline, critical path integration |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | PMB authorization, change control procedures for EVM baselines |
| P3 — Embed Quality | EVM data quality: accurate timephasing, work authorization, and progress measurement |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A16 — Financial Baseline and Funding Record | EVM PMB construction methodology applies to the financial baseline in A16 |
| A17 — Integrated Performance Reporting Record | EVM metrics (CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC) populate the performance report in A17 |
| SKL-05-01 — Report Project Performance | EVM metrics and reporting conventions are the core of this skill |
| SKL-03-07 — Develop Financial Baseline | PMB development process from EVM Standard informs this skill |

---

# REF-17 — Practice Standard for Scheduling (3rd ed.)
**Shortcode:** Scheduling-3E  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2019  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Schedule Model Development and Control  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2019). *Practice Standard for Scheduling* (3rd ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/scheduling-third-edition

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The Scheduling Standard provides the technical specifications for building, maintaining, and analyzing project schedule models — the operational depth that PMBOK 8 references but does not provide. It covers network diagramming conventions, activity sequencing logic types (FS, SS, FF, SF), critical path calculation, schedule compression techniques (fast-tracking, crashing), and schedule model quality criteria. It is the authority reference for any practitioner building a formal schedule model.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Scheduling-3E Contribution |
|---|---|
| Schedule Performance Domain (§2.3) | Schedule model construction, baseline, and control — technical specifications |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Schedule baseline governance: authorization, change control, variance thresholds |
| P3 — Embed Quality | Schedule model quality criteria: logic integrity, resource loading, constraint use |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A15 — Schedule Baseline and Forecast Record | Scheduling-3E model construction methods apply to A15 |
| SKL-03-06 — Develop Schedule Model and Baseline | Scheduling Standard is the primary technical reference for this skill |
| SKL-05-04 — Monitor and Control Schedule | Schedule variance analysis and compression methods from this standard apply |

---

**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2019). *Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures* (3rd ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/work-breakdown-structures-third-edition

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The WBS Standard provides the complete technical and practical guidance for developing, validating, and using Work Breakdown Structures. It defines WBS construction rules (the 100% rule, mutually exclusive work packages, decomposition depth guidance), WBS dictionary requirements, and industry-specific WBS examples. It is the definitive reference for scope decomposition — the foundation of all scope, schedule, and cost baseline integration.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | WBS-3E Contribution |
|---|---|
| Scope Performance Domain — Develop Scope Structure (§2.2.2.4) | WBS construction rules, decomposition techniques, and quality criteria |
| Schedule Performance Domain (§2.3) | WBS work packages as the foundation for activity definition and scheduling |
| Finance Performance Domain (§2.4) | WBS as the basis for cost account structure and bottom-up estimating |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A08 — Scope and Requirements Decision Record | WBS-3E decomposition model informs scope structure in A08 |
| SKL-03-05 — Define Scope | WBS construction rules from this standard are applied in this skill |

---

# REF-19 — Process Groups: A Practice Guide
**Shortcode:** Process-Groups  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2022  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Process Group Lifecycle Framework  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2022). *Process Groups: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/process-groups

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The Process Groups Practice Guide bridges the transition from PMBOK 6 (which used process groups and knowledge areas as its primary structure) to PMBOK 7/8 (which uses performance domains and principles). It organizes the 40 PMBOK 8 processes into five familiar process groups — Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing — providing a process-group-based view of project work for practitioners more comfortable with that mental model. It is the cross-reference map between PMBOK 8 and traditional process group thinking.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Process-Groups Contribution |
|---|---|
| All Performance Domains (§2.1–§2.7) | Maps all 40 PMBOK 8 processes to five process groups |
| Standard §4.5 — Focus Areas | Process groups align closely with PMBOK 8 focus areas (Initiating, Planning, Executing, M&C, Closing) |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | Process group view reinforces integrated project lifecycle thinking |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| LIFECYCLE-MAP.md | Process-Groups provides the lifecycle phase structure that LIFECYCLE-MAP.md is built upon |
| A41 — Process Inputs, Outputs, and Tools Reference Record | Process-Groups is a key source for I/O and T&T cross-references in A41 |
| All process records PR01–PR40 | Process-Groups provides cross-reference validation for all process record §Refs |

---
*Authority: PMI Process Groups Practice Guide (2022) · PMBOK8 §2, Standard §4.5*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-20 — Managing Change in Organizations
**Shortcode:** Change-Guide  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2013  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Organizational Change Management Integration  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2013). *Managing Change in Organizations: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/change

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide defines organizational change management (OCM) as a practice that integrates with — but is distinct from — project management. It provides the change readiness assessment model, stakeholder impact analysis, adoption planning, and resistance management techniques that support project benefit realization. PMBOK 8 covers change readiness as an artifact area (A29) but does not provide OCM methodology depth; this guide fills that gap.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Change-Guide Contribution |
|---|---|
| Stakeholders Performance Domain (§2.5) | Stakeholder change impact analysis and adoption planning techniques |
| P6 — Build an Empowered Culture | Change readiness as a cultural and leadership challenge |
| P2 — Focus on Value | Benefits only realized when organizational adoption occurs — OCM as a value enabler |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A29 — Change Readiness and Adoption Record | Change-Guide is the primary authority reference for A29 |
| SKL-04-04 — Manage Change Readiness and Adoption | OCM methodology from this guide informs all steps of this skill |
| SKL-06-03 — Transition Benefits to Business Owner | Change adoption confirmation is a closing prerequisite |

---
*Authority: PMI Managing Change in Organizations (2013) · PMBOK8 §2.5, §3.8*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-21 — Navigating Complexity: A Practice Guide
**Shortcode:** Complexity  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2014  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Complexity Assessment and Adaptive Response  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2014). *Navigating Complexity: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/complexity

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide provides a framework for identifying, assessing, and responding to project complexity — the conditions of uncertainty, ambiguity, dynamic change, and human behaviour interactions that make project outcomes difficult to predict. It defines four complexity dimensions (technical complexity, directional complexity, temporal complexity, social complexity) and recommends adaptive delivery and governance responses for each. It is the primary reference for tailoring decisions on high-complexity projects.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Complexity Contribution |
|---|---|
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | Complexity as a system-level property: requires systems thinking response |
| Development Approach (§2.3) | Complexity assessment as a key input to lifecycle selection |
| Risk Performance Domain (§2.7) | Complexity-induced uncertainty as a distinct risk category |
| P7 — Tailor Based on Context | Complexity dimensions inform tailoring decisions |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A06 — Tailoring Decision Record | Complexity assessment informs tailoring choices documented in A06 |
| A13 — Development Approach and Lifecycle Record | Complexity level is a primary input to lifecycle and approach selection |
| SKL-03-02 — Select Development Approach | Complexity dimensions from this guide inform adaptive vs. predictive selection |

---
*Authority: PMI Navigating Complexity (2014) · PMBOK8 §3.3, §3.9, §2.3*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-22 — Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects
**Shortcode:** Risk-PPP  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2024  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Risk Management Across All Delivery Tiers  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2024). *Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects: A Practice Guide*. Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/risk-management-in-portfolios

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
This guide extends PMBOK 8's project-level risk management framework to portfolio and program risk — covering enterprise risk appetite, risk aggregation across a portfolio, and how risk decisions at one level affect the others. For project practitioners, it provides an updated risk taxonomy, quantitative risk analysis techniques (Monte Carlo, sensitivity analysis, decision trees), and risk-informed decision-making tools that are more current than earlier PMI risk guidance.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Risk-PPP Contribution |
|---|---|
| Risk Performance Domain (§2.7) | Full risk process implementation: identification, qualitative/quantitative analysis, response, monitoring |
| Finance Performance Domain (§2.4) | Reserve analysis and contingency/management reserve calculation methods |
| Governance Performance Domain (§2.1) | Risk escalation governance, risk tolerance thresholds, and reserve authority |
| P1 — Adopt a Holistic View | Portfolio-level risk aggregation requires a holistic cross-project view |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| A19 — Risk Management Record | Risk-PPP is the primary authority for all risk identification and response content in A19 |
| A20 — Quantitative Risk and Reserve Record | Quantitative risk analysis methods from this guide populate A20 |
| SKL-03-11 — Identify and Analyze Risks | Risk-PPP identification and qualitative analysis techniques apply here |
| SKL-03-12 — Plan Risk Responses | Risk response strategy taxonomy from this guide informs this skill |
| SKL-04-08 — Implement Risk Responses | Risk-PPP monitoring and response adaptation applies during execution |

---
*Authority: PMI Risk Management in PPP (2024) · PMBOK8 §2.7*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-23 — PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms (5th ed.)
**Shortcode:** Lexicon  
**Publisher:** Project Management Institute (PMI), 2024  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Canonical PM Terminology  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Project Management Institute. (2024). *PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms* (5th ed.). Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/standards/lexicon

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The PMI Lexicon is the authoritative, edition-neutral glossary of project management terms across all PMI standards. Where PMBOK 8 provides a glossary limited to terms used within PMBOK 8 itself, the Lexicon covers terms across the entire PMI standards library — providing canonical definitions, disambiguation of similar terms, and cross-standard consistency. It is the reference of record when a term appears in multiple PMI documents with slightly different usage.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Lexicon Contribution |
|---|---|
| PMBOK8 Guide Glossary | Extends and disambiguates all PMBOK 8 glossary terms with cross-standard context |
| All Performance Domains | Provides canonical definitions for domain-specific terminology |
| All Principles | Provides canonical definitions for principle-related concepts |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| reference/GLOSSARY.md | The PMOSkills Glossary is harmonized with the PMI Lexicon as its external authority |
| All skill files | Term definitions in skill files must be consistent with the Lexicon |
| All process records | Process and artifact terminology must align with Lexicon canonical definitions |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- When a term's meaning is disputed or ambiguous, the Lexicon provides the PMI authoritative definition.
- For AI agents: the Lexicon is the disambiguation authority when processing PMI terminology across skills and process records.

---
*Authority: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms 5th ed. (2024) · PMBOK8 Glossary*  
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# REF-24 — Project Manager's Book of Forms
**Shortcode:** Forms-Book  
**Publisher:** John Wiley & Sons; Project Management Institute, 2017  
**Authority:** PMBOK8 Companion — Artifact Templates and Forms Library  
**Version:** 1.0.0 · **Status:** Active

## Full Bibliographic Citation
Dionisio, C. S. (2017). *Project Manager's Book of Forms: A Companion to the PMBOK® Guide*. John Wiley & Sons; Project Management Institute. Official release: https://www.pmi.org/shop/p-/book/a-project-manager's-book-of-forms---third-edition-(a-companion-to-the-pmbok-guide---sixth-edition)/00101606401

## What This Reference Adds Beyond PMBOK 8
The Book of Forms provides a complete library of pre-built project management document templates aligned to PMI standards. While PMOSkills provides its own artifact templates optimized for PMBOK 8, the Book of Forms serves as a cross-reference validation source — confirming what fields, sections, and governance elements should appear in standard PM documents. It is particularly useful for practitioners who need to align PMOSkills artifacts with widely-recognized template conventions.

## PMBOK 8 Domain and Principle Coverage

| PMBOK 8 Element | Forms-Book Contribution |
|---|---|
| All Performance Domains | Provides document templates for artifacts across all domains |
| P3 — Embed Quality | Template standards establish the minimum quality threshold for PM documentation |
| P4 — Be an Accountable Leader | Governance fields, approval signatures, and authority documentation in templates |

## PMOSkills Artifact and Skill Connections

| Item | Connection |
|---|---|
| All artifacts/ templates | Forms-Book serves as a cross-reference validation for artifact field completeness |
| reference/GLOSSARY.md | Forms-Book field definitions supplement the PMOSkills Glossary for documentation terminology |

## Practitioner Usage Notes
- Note: The Book of Forms was published as a companion to PMBOK 6. Field structures are still broadly applicable but some governance terminology reflects PMBOK 6 knowledge area framing. Cross-reference with PMBOK 8 when terminology differs.
- Use Forms-Book as a secondary validation source, not as a primary authority.

---

# Companion References Master Index

**Ref ID:** REF-COMP-INDEX  
**Type:** Index  
**PMBOK8 Source:** PMBOK 8 Core Standard · PMI Official Reference List  
**Version:** 1.0.0  
**Status:** Active  

---

## Purpose

This index serves as the **central directory mapping the 24 PMI companion references** to the core PMBOK 8th Edition Performance Domains and the skill layers in the PMOSkills repository. It enables practitioners to easily navigate the secondary literature and practice standards recommended by PMI for advanced project management execution.

---

## Companion References Directory

| ID | Title & Version | Shortcode | Core Performance Domain / Topic | Primary Skills Mapped |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **01** | **Agile Practice Guide** (2017) | `[APG]` | Development Approach & Life Cycle | Pack 07 Adaptive & Hybrid Skills |
| **02** | **Introduction to Disciplined Agile Delivery** (2nd ed.) | `[DAD-Intro]` | Development Approach & Life Cycle | Pack 07 Adaptive & Hybrid Skills |
| **03** | **AI Essentials for Project Professionals** (2024) | `[AI-Essentials]`| Technology & Uncertainty | reference/pmo/ (Cross-cutting guidance) |
| **04** | **Guide to Leading and Managing AI Projects** (2025) | `[AI-Projects]` | Technology & Uncertainty | reference/pmo/ (Cross-cutting guidance) |
| **05** | **Leading AI Transformation: Organizational Strategies** (2025)| `[AI-Transformation]`| Technology & Uncertainty | reference/pmo/ (Cross-cutting guidance) |
| **06** | **Enabling Project Management Transformation with GenAI** (2025)| `[GenAI-Transformation]`| Technology & Uncertainty | reference/pmo/ (Cross-cutting guidance) |
| **07** | **Business Analysis for Practitioners: A Practice Guide** (2nd ed.)| `[BAP-2E]` | Scope Domain / Business Analysis | Pack 03 Planning / Scope Skills |
| **08** | **The PMI Guide to Business Analysis** (2017) | `[PMI-BA]` | Scope Domain / Business Analysis | Pack 03 Planning / Scope Skills |
| **09** | **Requirements Management: A Practice Guide** (2016) | `[Requirements]` | Scope Domain / Requirements | Pack 03 Planning / Scope Skills |
| **10** | **Implementing Organizational Project Management** (2014) | `[OPM-Implementation]`| Governance Domain | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **11** | **Governance of Portfolios, Programs, and Projects** (2016) | `[GPPP]` | Governance Domain | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **12** | **The Standard for Organizational Project Management (OPM)** (2018)| `[OPM-Standard]` | Governance Domain | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **13** | **Project Management Offices: A Practice Guide** (2025) | `[PMO-Guide]` | Governance Domain | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **14** | **The Standard for Portfolio Management** (4th ed.) (2017) | `[Portfolio-4E]` | Portfolio Domain / PPM | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **15** | **The Standard for Program Management** (5th ed.) (2024) | `[Program-5E]` | Program Domain / PPM | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **16** | **The Standard for Earned Value Management** (2020) | `[EVM]` | Finance Domain / Cost | Pack 05 Monitoring & Controlling (EVM Skill) |
| **17** | **Practice Standard for Scheduling** (3rd ed.) (2019) | `[Scheduling-3E]` | Schedule Domain | Pack 03 Planning / Schedule Skills |
| **18** | **Practice Standard for Work Breakdown Structures** (3rd ed.) (2019)| `[WBS-3E]` | Scope Domain / WBS | Pack 03 Planning / Scope Skills |
| **19** | **Process Groups: A Practice Guide** (2022) | `[Process-Groups]` | Core Reference Processes | PR01–PR41 Processes |
| **20** | **Managing Change in Organizations: A Practice Guide** (2013) | `[Change-Guide]` | Change Management / Governance | Pack 08 PMO Governance Skills |
| **21** | **Navigating Complexity: A Practice Guide** (2014) | `[Complexity]` | Risk Domain / Uncertainty | Pack 03 Planning / Risk Skills |
| **22** | **Risk Management in Portfolios, Programs, and Projects** (2024) | `[Risk-PPP]` | Risk Domain / Uncertainty | Pack 03 Planning / Risk Skills |
| **23** | **PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms** (5th ed.) (2024) | `[Lexicon]` | Project Management Terms | Reference GLOSSARY |
| **24** | **Project Manager's Book of Forms** (2017) | `[Forms-Book]` | Forms-to-Artifact Mapping | Artifact Registry (A01–A41) |

---

## Mappings to PMBOK 8 Performance Domains

Each companion reference is mapped to a core domain in the PMBOK 8th Edition ecosystem:

### 1. Governance Performance Domain (PD8-01)
* **Primary References:** `[OPM-Implementation]`, `[GPPP]`, `[OPM-Standard]`, `[PMO-Guide]`, `[Change-Guide]`, `[Process-Groups]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR01 (Develop Charter), PR03 (Develop PMP), PR28 (Direct & Manage Work), PR29 (Manage Knowledge), PR30 (Monitor & Control Work), PR31 (Perform Integrated Change Control), PR40 (Close Project)
* **Description:** Governs project organization, lifecycle selection, tailoring, change control boards, and alignment.

### 2. Scope Performance Domain (PD8-02)
* **Primary References:** `[BAP-2E]`, `[PMI-BA]`, `[Requirements]`, `[WBS-3E]`, `[Forms-Book]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR04 (Scope Management), PR05 (Collect Requirements), PR06 (Define Scope), PR07 (Create WBS), PR32 (Validate Scope), PR33 (Control Scope)
* **Description:** Outlines requirements management, WBS decomposition, scope definition, and acceptance criteria.

### 3. Schedule Performance Domain (PD8-03)
* **Primary References:** `[Scheduling-3E]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR08 (Schedule Management), PR09 (Define Activities), PR10 (Sequence Activities), PR11 (Estimate Durations), PR12 (Develop Schedule), PR13 (Monitor Schedule), PR34 (Control Schedule)
* **Description:** Covers schedule modeling, activity duration estimation, critical path method, and variance control.

### 4. Finance Performance Domain (PD8-04)
* **Primary References:** `[EVM]`, `[Forms-Book]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR14 (Cost Management), PR15 (Estimate Costs), PR16 (Determine Budget), PR35 (Control Costs)
* **Description:** Details cost estimating techniques, budget baselining, reserve allocation, and earned value management calculations.

### 5. Stakeholders Performance Domain (PD8-05)
* **Primary References:** `[PMO-Guide]`, `[Lexicon]`, `[Forms-Book]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR02 (Identify Stakeholders), PR27 (Plan Stakeholder Engagement), PR36 (Monitor Communications)
* **Description:** Governs stakeholder identification, power/interest grids, communications strategy, and relationship monitoring.

### 6. Resources Performance Domain (PD8-06)
* **Primary References:** `[PMO-Guide]`, `[Lexicon]`, `[Forms-Book]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR18 (Plan Resource Management), PR19 (Estimate Activity Resources), PR38 (Control Resources)
* **Description:** Covers resource allocation, team charter/operating agreement, team development, and physical resource tracking.

### 7. Risk Performance Domain (PD8-07)
* **Primary References:** `[Risk-PPP]`, `[Complexity]`
* **Associated Processes:** PR21 (Plan Risk Management), PR22 (Identify Risks), PR23 (Qualitative Risk), PR24 (Quantitative Risk), PR25 (Plan Risk Responses), PR39 (Monitor Risks)
* **Description:** Details qualitative and quantitative risk analysis, risk response planning, reserve management, and complexity assessments.

---

*Authority: PMBOK8 Core Standard · PMI Official Reference List*

> NOTE: This index is intentionally maintained as a single file. Per-book companion reference pages will be added later where deeper implementation guidance is needed, after the Wave-based cleanup is complete.

# FA01 — Initiating
**Type:** Focus Area
**PMBOK8 Source:** Process Groups Practice Guide — Initiating
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
Initiating is the focus area in which the project or phase is formally defined and authorized. It establishes the project's existence within the organization, identifies the sponsoring authority, defines the high-level scope and objectives, and confirms the initial stakeholder landscape. The project officially begins here, and without the authorizations produced in this focus area, no subsequent work carries organizational legitimacy. Initiating applies both at project start and at the beginning of each major phase in multi-phase projects.

## Entry Conditions
- A business need, problem, opportunity, or strategic directive has been identified and assessed by a portfolio or sponsoring authority
- An organizational decision to initiate the project has been made and is documented
- Sufficient organizational context (enterprise environmental factors and organizational process assets) is available to evaluate feasibility
- A sponsor or authorizing executive is available and willing to commit resources and authority

## Primary Activities
- Develop or validate the Business Case to confirm the project's strategic alignment and value proposition
- Develop the Project Charter to formally authorize the project and define high-level scope, objectives, assumptions, constraints, and budget
- Formally assign the Project Manager with defined authority and accountability
- Identify stakeholders and populate the initial Stakeholder Register
- Establish initial governance structure and decision-authority framework
- Define high-level project objectives and success criteria
- Capture and document enterprise environmental factors and organizational process assets relevant to the project

## Primary Outputs / Artifacts Produced
| Artifact | Purpose |
|---|---|
| A01 — Business Case | Documents the justification for the project, confirming strategic alignment and expected benefits |
| A04 — Project Charter | Formally authorizes the project, assigns the PM, and defines high-level scope, objectives, and budget |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register (initial) | Records all identified stakeholders with initial interest, influence, and engagement assessments |
| A35 — Governance and Decision Authority Record (initial) | Establishes the initial governance structure, decision rights, and escalation paths |

## Artifacts Consumed (Inputs)
| Artifact | Source |
|---|---|
| A05 — Context Register (EEF/OPA capture) | Organizational process assets and enterprise environmental factors supplied by the sponsoring organization |
| Business need / strategic directive | Portfolio management decision or executive sponsorship authority |

## Skills Active in This Focus Area
| Skill ID | Skill Name |
|---|---|
| SKL-02-01 | Develop Project Charter |
| SKL-02-02 | Identify Stakeholders |

## Relationship to Performance Domains
Initiating activates a focused subset of performance domains at their earliest and most foundational level:

- **PD8-01 — Governance:** The project or phase is formally authorized (Initiate Project or Phase process), establishing the business case and governance boundaries.
- **PD8-05 — Stakeholders:** Initial stakeholder identification and analysis (Identify Stakeholders process) occurs, populating the initial Stakeholder Register.

All other performance domains (Scope, Schedule, Finance, Resources, Risk) are not yet formally active but may have early organizational inputs that inform the charter.

## Exit Criteria / Quality Gate
- Project Charter is signed by the authorizing sponsor
- Project Manager is formally named and has accepted the assignment with defined authority
- Initial budget or funding authorization is documented in the charter
- Initial Stakeholder Register is populated with at least the key stakeholders
- Governance structure and decision authority are documented
- Sponsor sign-off on all initiating artifacts is obtained
- Transition to FA02 Planning is explicitly authorized

*Authority: Process Groups Practice Guide — Initiating · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# FA02 — Planning
**Type:** Focus Area
**PMBOK8 Source:** Process Groups Practice Guide — Planning
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
Planning is the focus area in which the total scope of effort is established, all project objectives are detailed, and the integrated course of action for achieving those objectives is developed. Planning produces the baselines — scope, schedule, and financial — against which project performance will be measured throughout execution and monitoring. It also produces all subsidiary management plans that govern how specific knowledge areas (quality, risk, communications, procurement, stakeholder engagement) will be managed. Planning is iterative: plans are progressively elaborated and may be revisited as new information emerges, but the transition to execution requires an approved, integrated plan.

## Entry Conditions
- Project Charter is approved and signed
- Project Manager is formally assigned with defined authority
- Initial Stakeholder Register from FA01 is available
- Sponsor and key stakeholders are available to participate in requirements gathering and scope definition
- Organizational process assets and enterprise environmental factors have been documented in the Context Register (A05)

## Primary Activities
- Develop the Integrated Project Management Plan that integrates all subsidiary plans
- Define and decompose scope; create the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
- Collect, analyze, and document requirements; build the Requirements Traceability Matrix
- Develop the Schedule Baseline using sequencing, resource estimation, and duration analysis
- Develop the Financial Baseline through cost estimation and budget authorization
- Plan quality and define quality standards, metrics, and acceptance criteria
- Plan communications and establish the communications matrix
- Plan stakeholder engagement and develop targeted engagement strategies
- Plan risk management; identify, analyze (qualitative and quantitative), and plan risk responses
- Plan procurement; determine make-or-buy decisions and prepare procurement documentation
- Develop the Change Management Plan to govern benefit realization and organizational change
- Tailor the project management approach to the project's context, complexity, and delivery method
- Document team operating norms and resource capacity

## Primary Outputs / Artifacts Produced
| Artifact | Purpose |
|---|---|
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | The master governing document integrating all subsidiary plans and baselines; authorizes project execution |
| A15 — Schedule Baseline | Establishes the time-phased plan against which schedule performance is measured |
| A16 — Financial Baseline | Establishes the approved cost plan against which financial performance is measured |
| A08 — Scope and Requirements Backlog | Documents all validated requirements and scope boundaries; feeds the RTM |
| A09 — Quality Management Plan | Defines quality standards, metrics, assurance activities, and acceptance criteria |
| A10 — Communications Management Plan | Defines what information is communicated, to whom, when, and through what channels |
| A11 — Procurement Management Plan | Defines procurement strategy, vendor selection criteria, and contract types |
| A13 — Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) | Links requirements to deliverables and acceptance criteria to ensure complete coverage |
| A19 — Risk Management Record (initial) | Documents identified risks, probability-impact assessments, and planned risk responses |
| A25 — Team Operating Record | Documents team norms, roles, responsibilities, and operating agreements |
| A26 — Resource Capacity Record | Documents resource availability, capacity constraints, and allocation decisions |
| A39 — Change Management Plan | Defines how organizational change will be managed to support benefit realization |
| A06 — Tailoring Decision Record | Documents all tailoring decisions made to adapt the standard methodology to the project context |

## Artifacts Consumed (Inputs)
| Artifact | Source |
|---|---|
| A04 — Project Charter | FA01 Initiating; provides high-level scope, objectives, assumptions, constraints, and authorized budget |
| A01 — Business Case | FA01 Initiating; provides strategic context, benefit expectations, and success criteria |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | FA01 Initiating; identifies stakeholders whose requirements must be collected and whose engagement must be planned |
| A05 — Context Register | FA01 Initiating; provides EEFs and OPAs that constrain and enable planning decisions |

## Skills Active in This Focus Area
| Skill ID | Skill Name |
|---|---|
| SKL-03-01 | Develop Project Management Plan |
| SKL-03-02 | Plan Scope Management |
| SKL-03-03 | Collect Requirements |
| SKL-03-04 | Define Scope |
| SKL-03-05 | Create WBS |
| SKL-03-06 | Plan Schedule Management |
| SKL-03-07 | Define Activities |
| SKL-03-08 | Sequence Activities |
| SKL-03-09 | Estimate Activity Durations |
| SKL-03-10 | Develop Schedule |
| SKL-03-11 | Plan Cost Management |
| SKL-03-12 | Estimate Costs |
| SKL-03-13 | Determine Budget |
| SKL-03-14 | Plan Quality Management |
| SKL-03-15 | Plan Resource Management |
| SKL-03-16 | Plan Communications Management |
| SKL-03-17 | Plan Risk Management |

## Relationship to Performance Domains
Planning is the most multi-domain focus area; it directly engages and establishes the plans and baselines for all 7 performance domains:

- **PD8-01 — Governance:** Tailors the development approach and project lifecycle, plans sourcing strategies, and integrates all sub-plans into the master Integrated Project Management Plan.
- **PD8-02 — Scope:** Establishes scope boundaries, collects requirements, and decomposes scope into the WBS or backlog.
- **PD8-03 — Schedule:** Determines sequence, estimates activity durations, and develops the Schedule Baseline.
- **PD8-04 — Finance:** Plans financial management, estimates costs, and establishes the Financial Baseline (budget).
- **PD8-05 — Stakeholders:** Identifies stakeholder engagement plans and designs the communications approach.
- **PD8-06 — Resources:** Establishes resource plans, estimates resource requirements, and builds team operating charters.
- **PD8-07 — Risk:** Plans risk management, identifies risks, performs analysis, and prepares contingency reserves.

## Exit Criteria / Quality Gate
- Integrated Project Management Plan is reviewed and formally approved by the sponsor
- All three baselines (scope, schedule, financial) are approved and baselined
- All subsidiary management plans are complete and consistent with the integrated plan
- Risk management record contains identified and analyzed risks with documented response strategies
- RTM is populated and traceable from requirements to deliverables
- Tailoring decisions are documented and approved
- Planning complete gate is formally passed with sponsor authorization to proceed to execution

*Authority: Process Groups Practice Guide — Planning · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# FA03 — Executing
**Type:** Focus Area
**PMBOK8 Source:** Process Groups Practice Guide — Executing
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
Executing is the focus area in which the work defined in the approved Integrated Project Management Plan is directed, coordinated, and performed to produce the project deliverables. This focus area is where the plan becomes reality: the team is developed and managed, stakeholders are actively engaged, procurements are conducted, quality assurance activities are implemented, and project knowledge is captured and shared. Executing is the most resource-intensive focus area and operates in close, continuous feedback with FA04 Monitoring and Controlling to ensure that work remains aligned with approved plans and baselines.

## Entry Conditions
- Integrated Project Management Plan is formally approved by the sponsor
- All three baselines (scope, schedule, financial) are established and approved
- Project resources have been acquired or are committed and available
- Team operating agreements and role assignments from FA02 are in place
- Procurement vehicles (contracts, agreements) are executed or in process
- Kickoff meeting has been conducted and team is mobilized

## Primary Activities
- Direct and manage project work by authorizing work packages, coordinating team activities, and producing deliverables according to the schedule and scope baseline
- Manage project knowledge by capturing lessons learned, sharing expertise, and updating the Knowledge Record continuously
- Develop and manage the project team through coaching, conflict resolution, performance management, and skill development
- Manage stakeholder engagement by executing communication activities per the Communications Management Plan and responding to stakeholder needs and concerns
- Conduct procurements by executing vendor selection processes, awarding contracts, and managing vendor onboarding
- Manage quality by performing quality assurance activities, process audits, and ensuring deliverables meet defined acceptance criteria before submission
- Manage communications by producing, distributing, and archiving project information per the Communications Management Plan
- Implement change management activities from the Change Management Plan to support organizational readiness and benefit realization

## Primary Outputs / Artifacts Produced
| Artifact | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Project Deliverables (domain-specific) | The tangible products, services, or results produced by executing the work packages; the primary output of the project |
| A17 — Performance Reporting Record (updated) | Records actual performance data against baselines; feeds FA04 Monitoring and Controlling |
| A18 — Issue Log (updated) | Captures issues arising during execution that require resolution and tracks their status |
| A12 — Change Log (updated) | Records change requests raised during execution and their disposition |
| A30 — Knowledge Record (updated) | Captures lessons learned, best practices, and project knowledge as it emerges during execution |
| A29 — Change Readiness Record (updated) | Tracks organizational readiness for the changes the project will deliver; updated as change activities are implemented |

## Artifacts Consumed (Inputs)
| Artifact | Source |
|---|---|
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | FA02 Planning; the governing document that authorizes and directs all execution activities |
| A15 — Schedule Baseline | FA02 Planning; the time-phased work authorization and performance measurement reference |
| A16 — Financial Baseline | FA02 Planning; the cost authorization and financial performance reference |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | FA01/FA02; identifies stakeholders requiring active engagement during execution |
| A09 — Quality Management Plan | FA02 Planning; defines quality standards, assurance activities, and acceptance criteria |
| A19 — Risk Management Record | FA02 Planning; risk response plans that must be implemented or monitored during execution |

## Skills Active in This Focus Area
| Skill ID | Skill Name |
|---|---|
| SKL-04-01 | Direct and Manage Project Work |
| SKL-04-02 | Manage Project Knowledge |
| SKL-04-03 | Manage Quality |
| SKL-04-04 | Acquire Resources |
| SKL-04-05 | Develop Team |
| SKL-04-06 | Manage Team |
| SKL-04-07 | Manage Communications |
| SKL-04-08 | Implement Risk Responses |
| SKL-04-09 | Conduct Procurements |

## Relationship to Performance Domains
Executing is the focus area in which several key performance domains are active during task performance:

- **PD8-01 — Governance:** Directs and manages project execution, manages quality assurance audits, and coordinates lessons learned and project knowledge.
- **PD8-05 — Stakeholders:** Actively engages stakeholders, handles communication delivery, and resolves emergent stakeholder concerns.
- **PD8-06 — Resources:** Acquires necessary human and physical resources and leads/coaches the project team.
- **PD8-07 — Risk:** Implements agreed-upon risk responses to mitigate threats or capture opportunities.

## Exit Criteria / Quality Gate
- All planned deliverables have been produced and are available for formal acceptance review
- Deliverables meet defined quality standards and acceptance criteria documented in the RTM (A13)
- No unresolved blocking issues remain in the Issue Log (A18)
- All approved change requests have been implemented or are formally deferred
- Knowledge Record (A30) reflects all material lessons learned from execution
- Performance data has been reported and is current in the Performance Reporting Record (A17)
- Sponsor or designated acceptance authority confirms readiness for closure review

*Authority: Process Groups Practice Guide — Executing · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# FA04 — Monitoring and Controlling
**Type:** Focus Area
**PMBOK8 Source:** Process Groups Practice Guide — Monitoring and Controlling
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
Monitoring and Controlling is the focus area that tracks, reviews, and regulates the progress and performance of the project against the approved baselines. It identifies any areas in which changes to the plan are required and initiates the corresponding corrective, preventive, or change-control actions. Unlike the other focus areas, Monitoring and Controlling does not occur at a single point in the lifecycle — it runs concurrently with all other focus areas from project initiation through closure. This continuous nature makes it the feedback and governance engine of the project, ensuring that deviations are detected early, decisions are made with current data, and the project remains aligned with authorized scope, schedule, cost, and quality targets.

## Entry Conditions
- Project execution (FA03) has begun and work is producing measurable outputs
- Performance baselines (scope, schedule, financial) are approved and established
- Performance reporting cadence and thresholds are defined in the Integrated PMP (A14)
- A change control process and Change Log (A12) are in place
- Risk monitoring responsibilities are assigned

## Primary Activities
- Monitor and control project work by collecting performance data, analyzing variances, and reporting status against all baselines
- Perform integrated change control by reviewing all change requests, assessing impacts, and approving or rejecting changes through the change control board or designated authority
- Control scope by comparing delivered scope against the scope baseline and managing scope creep
- Control schedule by analyzing schedule performance indices, identifying critical path impacts, and initiating corrective actions
- Control costs by analyzing cost performance indices, earned value metrics, and financial forecast variances
- Control quality by inspecting deliverables against acceptance criteria, performing testing, and managing defect resolution
- Monitor stakeholder engagement by assessing whether stakeholder satisfaction and engagement levels remain aligned with the communications plan
- Monitor risks by reviewing the Risk Management Record, tracking risk triggers, implementing contingency plans, and identifying new risks
- Control procurements by reviewing vendor performance against contract terms, managing claims, and overseeing contract changes

## Primary Outputs / Artifacts Produced
| Artifact | Purpose |
|---|---|
| A17 — Performance Reporting Record (updated each cycle) | Records current performance status, variance analysis, and forecasts; the primary communication vehicle for project health |
| A12 — Change Log (change decisions recorded) | Documents all change requests, impact assessments, approval/rejection decisions, and implementation status |
| A18 — Issue Log (updated) | Tracks open issues identified through monitoring, their resolution actions, owners, and closure status |
| A19 — Risk Management Record (risk response tracking) | Updated with risk status, trigger observations, response execution records, and newly identified risks |
| A20 — Quantitative Risk Reserve Decision Record (reserve drawdowns) | Records decisions to draw down contingency or management reserves and the justification for each drawdown |

## Artifacts Consumed (Inputs)
| Artifact | Source |
|---|---|
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | FA02 Planning; defines the baselines and thresholds against which performance is measured |
| A15 — Schedule Baseline | FA02 Planning; the authorized schedule against which actual progress and EV calculations are made |
| A16 — Financial Baseline | FA02 Planning; the authorized cost plan against which actual spend and EAC forecasts are compared |
| A13 — Requirements Traceability Matrix | FA02 Planning; used in scope and quality control to verify all requirements are being addressed |
| A09 — Quality Management Plan | FA02 Planning; defines acceptance criteria and quality thresholds used in quality control activities |

## Skills Active in This Focus Area
| Skill ID | Skill Name |
|---|---|
| SKL-05-01 | Monitor and Control Project Work |
| SKL-05-02 | Perform Integrated Change Control |
| SKL-05-03 | Validate Scope |
| SKL-05-04 | Control Scope |
| SKL-05-05 | Control Schedule |
| SKL-05-06 | Control Costs |
| SKL-05-07 | Control Quality |
| SKL-05-08 | Monitor Stakeholder Engagement |
| SKL-05-09 | Monitor Risks |

## Relationship to Performance Domains
Monitoring and Controlling serves as the governance overlay that connects and monitors progress across all 7 performance domains:

- **PD8-01 — Governance:** Regulates project performance, conducts change control, and manages knowledge audits.
- **PD8-02 — Scope:** Inspects deliverables against quality requirements and controls scope creep (Control Scope and Validate Scope processes).
- **PD8-03 — Schedule:** Monitors activity progress and critical path deviations against the schedule baseline.
- **PD8-04 — Finance:** Tracks expenditures, calculates earned value indices (CV, SV, CPI, SPI), and forecasts financial completion.
- **PD8-05 — Stakeholders:** Assesses stakeholder engagement levels and monitors communications performance.
- **PD8-06 — Resources:** Monitors team performance, resource usage, and supplier/procurement contract compliance.
- **PD8-07 — Risk:** Monitors risk triggers, implements contingency responses, and updates the risk register.

## Exit Criteria / Quality Gate
- All planned deliverables are formally accepted by the sponsor or designated acceptance authority
- All performance variances are within approved tolerance bands or have approved corrective action plans
- No open critical issues remain in the Issue Log (A18)
- All change requests are resolved (approved, rejected, or deferred) with documented decisions in the Change Log (A12)
- Risk posture is documented as acceptable; all critical risks are closed or have active response plans
- Final performance reports are current and complete
- Closure is formally authorized by the sponsor; transition to FA05 Closing is approved

*Authority: Process Groups Practice Guide — Monitoring and Controlling · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*
