# FA05 — Closing
**Type:** Focus Area
**PMBOK8 Source:** Process Groups Practice Guide — Closing
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
Closing is the focus area in which all project activities are formally completed, final acceptance is obtained, deliverables and benefits are transferred to the receiving organization, resources are released, and organizational memory is captured and archived. Closing is not merely administrative finalization — it is the structured handover that ensures the benefits the project was chartered to deliver are transitioned and owned by the business. It also preserves institutional knowledge through lessons learned consolidation and records archival, protecting the organization's future capability. Closing applies at the end of the full project and, in multi-phase projects, at the conclusion of each phase.

## Entry Conditions
- All project deliverables have been formally accepted by the sponsor or designated acceptance authority
- Sponsor and key stakeholder acceptance is documented and obtained
- All procurement contracts have been administered, closed, or are ready for final closure
- All open issues in the Issue Log (A18) are resolved or formally accepted as residual
- Financial closure prerequisites are met (all invoices processed, final costs confirmed)
- Authorization to close has been granted through FA04 exit criteria

## Primary Activities
- Obtain final product, service, or result acceptance from the sponsor or client, documented with formal sign-off
- Finalize and close all procurement contracts; confirm supplier obligations are fulfilled; release vendor relationships
- Release team members and resources formally; conduct performance evaluations and transition team to other assignments
- Document and consolidate final lessons learned from all project phases into the Lessons Learned Record (A21)
- Archive all project records per the Information Retention Record (A34) and organizational retention policies
- Complete financial closure by reconciling final actuals, releasing unused budget, and submitting final financial reports
- Transfer benefits to the business owner per the Benefits Realization Plan; confirm benefit ownership and measurement responsibilities post-project
- Produce the final performance report summarizing actual vs. planned performance across scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk
- Conduct the project closure review (post-project review) to assess team performance, process effectiveness, and organizational learning

## Primary Outputs / Artifacts Produced
| Artifact | Purpose |
|---|---|
| A27 — Project Closure Record | The formal document that declares the project closed, records final performance summary, and captures sponsor sign-off |
| A21 — Lessons Learned Record (final consolidation) | Consolidates all lessons learned from all phases into a single organizational learning asset |
| A24 — Closure and Benefits Transition Record | Documents the formal transfer of deliverables and benefit ownership to the receiving business unit |
| A34 — Information Retention Record (archival actions) | Documents which records are archived, where, for how long, and under what access controls |

## Artifacts Consumed (Inputs)
| Artifact | Source |
|---|---|
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | FA02 Planning; the governing document against which final performance is assessed and closure completeness is verified |
| A13 — Requirements Traceability Matrix (all requirements accepted) | FA02 Planning; confirms that all requirements have been delivered and accepted before formal closure |
| A17 — Performance Reports (final) | FA04 Monitoring and Controlling; provides the cumulative performance record used in the final report and closure review |
| A30 — Knowledge Record (consolidated) | FA03 Executing; feeds the final Lessons Learned consolidation with execution-phase insights |
| A19 — Risk Management Record (closed risks) | FA04 Monitoring and Controlling; documents risk closure status and residual risk transfers to operations |

## Skills Active in This Focus Area
| Skill ID | Skill Name |
|---|---|
| SKL-06-01 | Close Project or Phase |
| SKL-06-02 | Final Performance Review |
| SKL-06-03 | Conduct Post-Project Review |

## Relationship to Performance Domains
Closing engages several key performance domains at their terminal and reflective stages:

- **PD8-01 — Governance:** The primary domain for closing; executes the Close Project or Phase process, formalizes organizational archives, reconciles budgets, and completes contract closures.
- **PD8-02 — Scope:** Confirms that all scope requirements have been delivered and accepted (Validate Scope output transition).
- **PD8-05 — Stakeholders:** Assesses final stakeholder satisfaction and obtains formal sponsor sign-off.
- **PD8-06 — Resources:** Releases team members and physical resources, and conducts team evaluations.
- **PD8-07 — Risk:** Identifies residual risks to transition to operations and closes the risk register.

## Exit Criteria / Quality Gate
- Project Closure Record (A27) is completed and signed by the sponsor
- All deliverables formally accepted and transition confirmed in the Closure and Benefits Transition Record (A24)
- All procurement contracts formally closed with no outstanding claims
- Lessons Learned Record (A21) finalized and submitted to organizational knowledge repository
- All project records archived per the Information Retention Record (A34)
- Financial accounts closed; final financial report submitted and accepted
- Benefits transferred per A24 with named operational owner and ongoing measurement plan
- All team members formally released and reassigned
- Post-project review completed and findings distributed to the PMO or sponsoring organization

*Authority: Process Groups Practice Guide — Closing · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

reference/inputs-outputs/inputs-outputs-.rawContent:
--- ref_id: IO-REGISTRY ref_name: "Inputs and Outputs Master Registry" version: "1.0.0" status: Complete authority: PMBOK8 Primary pmbok8_source: "PMBOK8 Guide §2 · Process Groups: A Practice Guide §2" file_path: "reference/inputs-outputs/inputs-outputs-registry.md" migrated_from: "C7-IO-Registry/IO-Registry-Index.md" migration_date: "2026-06-02" migration_phase: "Phase 4 — C7" --- # Inputs & Outputs Master Registry **Ref ID:** IO-REGISTRY **Type:** Registry **PMBOK8 Source:** PMBOK 8 Performance Domains · Process Groups: A Practice Guide §4–§12 **Version:** 1.0.0 **Status:** Complete --- ## Purpose This registry serves as the **authoritative, deduplicated master catalog of all project inputs and outputs** across the 41 process records (**PR01–PR41**) in `reference/processes/`. Each project plan, baseline, document, register, or data stream is classified, defined, and mapped back to the exact process that produces it and the processes that consume it. --- ## Artifact Classification System All project artifacts are categorized using the following standardized type codes: | Code | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **PL** | Subsidiary Plan | Management plans that form components of the Project Management Plan (PMP). | | **BL** | Baseline | Change-controlled baseline (Scope, Schedule, Cost, or PMB). | | **DOC** | Project Document | Dynamic working files updated throughout the project lifecycle. | | **REG** | Register | Structured logs that accumulate and track historical/living details. | | **REP** | Performance Report | Formally formatted summaries of status, metrics, and forecasts. | | **REQ** | Request / Decision | Change requests, approved changes, or defect repair instructions. | | **DATA** | Performance Data | Raw data, analyzed performance information, and metrics. | | **ENV** | Enterprise / Org Asset | EEFs and OPAs — external conditions or organizational assets. | | **BUS** | Business Document | Strategic justifications (Business Case, Benefits Management Plan). | | **OUT** | Primary Output | Main physical deliverables or final transition results. | --- ## Section 1 — Subsidiary Management Plans (PL) Subsidiary plans form the component building blocks of the comprehensive **A14 Integrated Project Management Plan (PMP)**. | Plan Component | Type | Produced By | Consumed As Input By | |---|---|---|---| | **Scope Management Plan** | PL | PR04 Plan Scope | PR03, PR05, PR06, PR07, PR32, PR33 | | **Requirements Management Plan**| PL | PR04 Plan Scope | PR03, PR05, PR33 | | **Schedule Management Plan** | PL | PR08 Plan Schedule | PR03, PR09, PR10, PR11, PR12, PR34 | | **Cost Management Plan** | PL | PR14 Plan Cost | PR03, PR15, PR16, PR35 | | **Quality Management Plan** | PL | PR17 Plan Quality | PR03, PR37 | | **Resource Management Plan** | PL | PR18 Plan Resource | PR03, PR19, PR38 | | **Communications Management Plan**| PL | PR20 Plan Comm | PR03, PR36 | | **Risk Management Plan** | PL | PR21 Plan Risk | PR03, PR22, PR23, PR24, PR25, PR39 | | **Procurement Management Plan** | PL | PR26 Plan Procurement| PR03, PR41 | | **Stakeholder Engagement Plan** | PL | PR27 Plan Stakeholder | PR02, PR03 | | **Change Management Plan** | PL | PR03 Develop PMP | PR31 Perform Change Control | | **Configuration Management Plan**| PL | PR03 Develop PMP | PR31 Perform Change Control | --- ## Section 2 — Project Baselines (BL) Baselines represent change-controlled standards against which project execution is measured. | Baseline | Type | Produced By | Consumed As Input By | |---|---|---|---| | **Scope Baseline**
*(Scope Statement + WBS + Dictionary)* | BL | PR07 Create WBS | PR09, PR11, PR12, PR15, PR16, PR32, PR33 | | **Schedule Baseline (A15)** | BL | PR12 Develop Schedule| PR13, PR34, PR35 | | **Cost Baseline (A16)** | BL | PR16 Determine Budget| PR35 | | **Performance Measurement Baseline**| BL | PR03 Develop PMP | PR30 Monitor & Control Work | --- ## Section 3 — Project Documents (DOC) Dynamic files that record specific data points and are elaborately updated. | Document | Type | Produced By | Consumed As Input By | |---|---|---|---| | **Project Charter (A04)** | DOC | PR01 Develop Charter | PR02, PR03, PR04, PR08, PR14, PR17, PR18, PR20, PR21, PR26, PR27 | | **Project Scope Statement** | DOC | PR06 Define Scope | PR07 | | **Requirements Documentation** | DOC | PR05 Collect Req | PR06, PR07, PR17, PR26, PR32, PR37 | | **Requirements Traceability Matrix**| DOC | PR05 Collect Req | PR32, PR37 | | **Activity List** | DOC | PR09 Define Activities| PR10, PR11, PR12, PR19 | | **Activity Attributes** | DOC | PR09 Define Activities| PR10, PR11, PR12, PR19 | | **Milestone List** | DOC | PR09 Define Activities| PR10, PR12 | | **Schedule Network Diagrams** | DOC | PR10 Sequence Act | PR12 | | **Duration Estimates** | DOC | PR11 Estimate Durations| PR12 | | **Basis of Estimates** | DOC | PR11 & PR15 | PR12, PR16 | | **Project Schedule** | DOC | PR12 Develop Schedule| PR13, PR30, PR34, PR38 | | **Project Calendars** | DOC | PR12 Develop Schedule| PR19 | | **Cost Estimates** | DOC | PR15 Estimate Costs | PR16 | | **Funding Requirements** | DOC | PR16 Determine Budget| PR35 | | **Quality Metrics** | DOC | PR17 Plan Quality | PR37 | | **Resource Requirements** | DOC | PR19 Estimate Res | PR11, PR12, PR15, PR16, PR38 | | **Resource Breakdown Structure** | DOC | PR19 Estimate Res | PR38 | | **Resource Calendars** | DOC | PR19 Estimate Res | PR12 | | **Project Team Assignments** | DOC | PR18 Plan Resource | PR38 | | **Agreements / Contracts** | DOC | External (or PR26) | PR40, PR41 | | **Procurement SOW & Bid Docs** | DOC | PR26 Plan Procurement| PR41 | --- ## Section 4 — Living Registers (REG) Logs that accumulate entries and track real-time project states. | Register | Type | Created By | Updated Throughout / Consumed By | |---|---|---|---| | **Stakeholder Register (A07)** | REG | PR02 Identify Stakeholder| PR03, PR05, PR20, PR21, PR27, PR36 | | **Risk Register (A19)** | REG | PR22 Identify Risks | PR23, PR24, PR25, PR30, PR39 | | **Assumption Log** | REG | PR01 Develop Charter | PR03, PR05, PR06, PR09, PR11, PR15, PR22 | | **Issue Log** | REG | PR28 Direct & Manage | PR30, PR36, PR38, PR39 | | **Lessons Learned Register** | REG | PR29 Manage Knowledge| PR03, PR30, PR40 | | **Change Log** | REG | PR31 Perform ICC | PR30, PR33, PR34, PR35 | --- ## Section 5 — Performance Reports (REP) Formatted records detailing metrics, variances, and forecasts. | Report | Type | Produced By | Consumed As Input By | |---|---|---|---| | **Work Performance Reports** | REP | PR30 Monitor & Control | PR31 ICC, PR36, PR38, PR39 | | **Quality Reports** | REP | PR37 Control Quality | PR30 Monitor & Control | | **Risk Report** | REP | PR22 Identify Risks | PR23, PR24, PR25, PR30, PR39 | | **Schedule Forecasts** | REP | PR34 Control Schedule| PR30 Monitor & Control | | **Cost Forecasts** | REP | PR35 Control Costs | PR30 Monitor & Control | | **Final Project Report** | REP | PR40 Close Project | Sponsor, Customer, PMO Archives | --- ## Section 6 — Performance Data Flow (DATA) The structural data flow that bridges execution to monitoring, analysis, and reporting. | Data Stage | Type | Originating Process | Consumed By | |---|---|---|---| | **Work Performance Data** | DATA | PR28 Direct & Manage | M&C Processes (`PR32–PR39`, `PR41`) | | **Work Performance Info** | DATA | M&C Processes (`PR32–PR39`, `PR41`) | PR30 Monitor & Control Project Work | | **Work Performance Reports** | DATA | PR30 Monitor & Control | PR31 Perform ICC (Change Control) | --- ## Section 7 — Requests, Repairs, and Decisions (REQ) Formal actions seeking modifications or documenting approvals. | Request Type | Type | Produced By | Consumed / Approved By | |---|---|---|---| | **Change Requests** | REQ | Almost all processes | PR31 Perform Integrated Change Control | | **Approved Change Requests** | REQ | PR31 Perform ICC | PR28 Direct and Manage Project Work | | **Defect Repairs** | REQ | PR37 Control Quality | PR28 Direct and Manage Project Work | --- ## Section 8 — Strategic & Business Documents (BUS) Inputs representing business goals and investment boundaries. | Document | Type | Produced By | Consumed As Input By | |---|---|---|---| | **Business Case** | BUS | Enterprise | PR01 Develop Charter, PR03, PR40 | | **Benefits Management Plan** | BUS | Enterprise | PR01 Develop Charter, PR03, PR40 | --- ## Section 9 — Primary Deliverables & Transitions (OUT) Physical deliverable handoffs representing value realization. | Deliverable State | Type | Originating Process | Transition Gate Process | |---|---|---|---| | **Deliverables (Raw)** | OUT | PR28 Direct & Manage | PR37 Control Quality (for verification) | | **Verified Deliverables** | OUT | PR37 Control Quality | PR32 Validate Scope (for acceptance) | | **Accepted Deliverables** | OUT | PR32 Validate Scope | PR40 Close Project (for transition) | | **Final Product/Service Handoff**| OUT | PR40 Close Project | Sponsor / Operations Transition | --- ## Master Artifact Usage Frequency | Rank | Artifact Name | Process Input Count | Primary Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEF)** | 41 | ENV | | 2 | **Organizational Process Assets (OPA)** | 41 | ENV | | 3 | **Integrated Project Management Plan (PMP)** | 32 | PL | | 4 | **Risk Register** | 12 | REG | | 5 | **Stakeholder Register** | 8 | REG | | 6 | **Project Charter** | 11 | DOC | | 7 | **Assumption Log** | 7 | REG | | 8 | **Scope Baseline** | 7 | BL | | 9 | **Work Performance Data** | 9 | DATA | | 10 | **Work Performance Information** | 8 | DATA | --- ## Core Operational Chains ### 1. Scope and WBS Baseline Chain ``` PR01 Charter → PR05 Collect Requirements (creates Requirements Doc & RTM) → PR06 Define Scope (creates Scope Statement) → PR07 Create WBS (creates WBS Dictionary & Scope Baseline) → PR09 Define Activities (Activity List) → PR10 Sequence Activities → PR12 Develop Schedule (creates Schedule Baseline) ``` ### 2. Work Performance Data-to-Report Chain ``` PR28 Direct & Manage (produces raw Work Performance Data & Deliverables) → PR32-39, PR41 M&C Processes (analyze data to produce Work Performance Info) → PR30 Monitor & Control (compiles Info into Work Performance Reports) → PR31 Perform ICC (evaluates Reports for Change Requests) → PR28 Executing (applies Approved Change Requests to work) ``` ### 3. Quality Verification & Customer Acceptance Chain ``` PR28 Direct & Manage (produces raw Deliverables) → PR37 Control Quality (checks deliverables to produce Verified Deliverables) → PR32 Validate Scope (customer reviews to produce Accepted Deliverables) → PR40 Close Project (transitions deliverables to produce Final Transition) ``` --- *Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2 · Process Groups Practice Guide §4–§12 · PMOSkills Repository*

# Knowledge Area Cross-Reference Index

**Ref ID:** KA-CROSSREF-INDEX  
**Type:** Index  
**PMBOK8 Source:** PMBOK 8 Performance Domains · Process Groups: A Practice Guide §2  
**Version:** 1.0.0  
**Status:** Complete  

---

## Purpose

This index provides a single, authoritative cross-reference mapping every PMBOK process record (**PR01–PR41**) maintained in `reference/processes/` to its respective **PMI Knowledge Area** (what domain of project management knowledge it addresses) and **Process Group** (when in the project lifecycle it is executed).

Use this index to:
- Navigate from any of the 10 Knowledge Areas to its respective detailed process records.
- Understand how processes flow within and across project lifecycle stages.
- Track primary outputs and baselines to their originating source processes.
- Facilitate quick routing of process-related queries for practitioners and AI agents.

---

## Process Summary by Knowledge Area

### KA1 — Project Integration Management
Integration Management coordinates and aligns project activities, deliverables, and lifecycle phases. It spans all five Process Groups.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR01 | Develop Project Charter | Initiating | A04 Project Charter, Assumption Log |
| PR03 | Develop Project Management Plan | Planning | A14 Integrated PMP |
| PR28 | Direct and Manage Project Work | Executing | Deliverables, Work Performance Data, Issue Log |
| PR29 | Manage Project Knowledge | Executing | Lessons Learned Register, OPA Updates |
| PR30 | Monitor and Control Project Work | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Reports, Change Requests |
| PR31 | Perform Integrated Change Control | Monitoring & Controlling | Approved Change Requests, PMP Updates |
| PR40 | Close Project or Phase | Closing | Final Project Report, Product Transition, OPA Updates |

* **Process Count:** 7
* **Practitioner Note:** Integration Management acts as the core spine of project delivery; all change requests and performance reports flow through here.

---

### KA2 — Project Scope Management
Scope Management ensures the project includes all the work required, and only the work required, to complete the project successfully.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR04 | Plan Scope Management | Planning | Scope Management Plan, Requirements Plan |
| PR05 | Collect Requirements | Planning | Requirements Documentation, A13 RTM |
| PR06 | Define Scope | Planning | Project Scope Statement |
| PR07 | Create WBS | Planning | Scope Baseline (WBS + WBS Dictionary) |
| PR32 | Validate Scope | Monitoring & Controlling | Accepted Deliverables |
| PR33 | Control Scope | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Info, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 6
* **Key Baseline:** Scope Baseline (created in PR07, validated in PR32, controlled in PR33).

---

### KA3 — Project Schedule Management
Schedule Management establishes policies, network structures, durations, and baselines to manage timely project completion.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR08 | Plan Schedule Management | Planning | Schedule Management Plan |
| PR09 | Define Activities | Planning | Activity List, Milestone List |
| PR10 | Sequence Activities | Planning | Schedule Network Diagram |
| PR11 | Estimate Activity Durations | Planning | Duration Estimates, Basis of Estimates |
| PR12 | Develop Schedule | Planning | A15 Schedule Baseline, Project Schedule |
| PR13 | Monitor and Control Schedule | Planning | Schedule Forecasts, Work Performance Info |
| PR34 | Control Schedule | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Info, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 7
* **Key Baseline:** Schedule Baseline (created in PR12, controlled in PR34).

---

### KA4 — Project Cost Management
Cost Management involves estimating, budgeting, financing, and controlling costs so the project can be completed within the approved budget.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR14 | Plan Cost Management | Planning | Cost Management Plan |
| PR15 | Estimate Costs | Planning | Cost Estimates, Basis of Estimates |
| PR16 | Determine Budget | Planning | A16 Cost Baseline, Project Funding Requirements |
| PR35 | Control Costs | Monitoring & Controlling | Cost Forecasts, Work Performance Info |

* **Process Count:** 4
* **Key Baseline:** Cost Baseline (created in PR16, controlled in PR35).

---

### KA5 — Project Quality Management
Quality Management incorporates the organization's quality policies to ensure project deliverables meet standard specifications and customer requirements.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR17 | Plan Quality Management | Planning | Quality Management Plan, Quality Metrics |
| PR37 | Control Quality | Monitoring & Controlling | Verified Deliverables, Quality Control Measurements |

* **Process Count:** 2
* **Note:** Quality Control verifies work deliverables (PR37) before they are sent to the customer for formal acceptance in Validate Scope (PR32).

---

### KA6 — Project Resource Management
Resource Management identifies, estimates, acquires, and manages team and physical resources needed for project success.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR18 | Plan Resource Management | Planning | Resource Management Plan, Team Charter |
| PR19 | Estimate Activity Resources | Planning | Resource Requirements, Resource Breakdown Structure (RBS) |
| PR38 | Control Resources | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Info, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 3
* **Note:** Covers team organization, physical equipment tracking, resource estimates, and utilization monitoring.

---

### KA7 — Project Communications Management
Communications Management ensures timely planning, collection, creation, distribution, storage, and monitoring of project information.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR20 | Plan Communications Management | Planning | Communications Management Plan |
| PR36 | Monitor Communications | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Info, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 2
* **Core Goal:** Delivers the right message to the right stakeholder in the right format at the right time.

---

### KA8 — Project Risk Management
Risk Management identifies, analyzes, and plans risk responses to increase positive opportunities and minimize negative threats.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR21 | Plan Risk Management | Planning | Risk Management Plan |
| PR22 | Identify Risks | Planning | A19 Risk Register, Risk Report |
| PR23 | Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis | Planning | Prioritized Risk Register |
| PR24 | Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis | Planning | Numerical Risk Exposure Estimates |
| PR25 | Plan Risk Responses | Planning | Risk Response Strategies, Change Requests |
| PR39 | Monitor Risks | Monitoring & Controlling | Work Performance Info, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 6
* **Key Flow:** Identify (PR22) → Analyze (PR23, PR24) → Respond (PR25) → Monitor (PR39).

---

### KA9 — Project Procurement Management
Procurement Management handles contract generation, supplier evaluations, claims administration, and closeouts for external resources and vendors.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR26 | Plan Procurement Management | Planning | A11 Procurement Plan, SOW, Source Selection Criteria |
| PR41 | Control Procurements | Monitoring & Controlling | Closed Procurements, Claims Decisions, Change Requests |

* **Process Count:** 2
* **Procurement Cycle:** Plan (PR26) → Execute/Award → Monitor/Close (PR41).

---

### KA10 — Project Stakeholder Management
Stakeholder Management identifies all parties impacted by the project and builds strategic engagement plans to maximize positive support.

| ID | Process Name | Process Group | Primary Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| PR02 | Identify Stakeholders | Initiating | A07 Stakeholder Register, Change Requests |
| PR27 | Plan Stakeholder Engagement | Planning | Stakeholder Engagement Plan |

* **Process Count:** 2
* **Stakeholder Circle:** Identify (PR02) → Strategy Plan (PR27).

---

---

## Process Distribution Matrix

| Knowledge Area | Initiating | Planning | Executing | M&C | Closing | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Integration | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | **7** |
| 2. Scope | — | 4 | — | 2 | — | **6** |
| 3. Schedule | — | 6 | — | 1 | — | **7** |
| 4. Cost | — | 3 | — | 1 | — | **4** |
| 5. Quality | — | 1 | — | 1 | — | **2** |
| 6. Resource | — | 2 | — | 1 | — | **3** |

| 10. Stakeholder | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | **2** |
| **TOTAL** | **2** | **25** | **2** | **11** | **1** | **41** |

---

## Baseline and PMB Traceability

The Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB) is formed by combining the following three key baselines:

| Baseline | Originating Process | Control Process | Associated Artifacts |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope Baseline** | PR07 Create WBS | PR33 Control Scope | Scope Statement, WBS, WBS Dictionary |
| **Schedule Baseline** | PR12 Develop Schedule | PR34 Control Schedule | Schedule Baseline (A15), Gantt Charts |
| **Cost Baseline** | PR16 Determine Budget | PR35 Control Costs | Cost Baseline (A16), Funding Requirements |

All changes impacting these baselines must be approved via **PR31 (Perform Integrated Change Control)**.

---

# PD8-01 — Governance Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1 — Governance Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Governance Performance Domain** addresses the framework, functions, and processes that guide project management decisions and activities to optimize the project's value delivery (PMBOK8 §2.1). This domain integrates elements of project integration management related to strategic alignment, decision-making, change control, and project success criteria. It coordinates various processes and documents as an integrated system for value delivery.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.1, effective execution of the Governance Performance Domain results in:
- **Strategic alignment** of the project or phase with organizational goals, portfolio priorities, and business strategies.
- **Appropriate decision-making frameworks** tailored to project complexity, protecting project investments and resource allocations.
- **Robust change control mechanisms** that evaluate scope, schedule, and cost impacts against the project's value proposition.
- **Project success criteria** that are defined, measured, and validated throughout the project lifecycle.
- **Effective sourcing strategies** aligned with the project's delivery approach and organizational policies.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 9 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.1.6):
1. **Initiate Project or Phase** (§2.1.6.1) — Formally authorizes the work, establishes the business case, and identifies initial boundaries.
2. **Integrate and Align Project Plans** (§2.1.6.2) — Consolidated coordination of all sub-plans and baselines into a unified project management plan.
3. **Plan Sourcing Strategy** (§2.1.6.3) — Determines whether to buy, rent, or build resources and details the procurement approach.
4. **Manage Project Execution** (§2.1.6.4) — Directs and carries out the work defined in the plans to achieve deliverables and outcomes.
5. **Manage Quality Assurance** (§2.1.6.5) — Audits quality requirements and the results from quality control measurements to ensure appropriate processes are followed.
6. **Manage Project Knowledge** (§2.1.6.6) — Fosters a learning environment to share and generate knowledge throughout the project.
7. **Monitor and Control Project Performance** (§2.1.6.7) — Tracks, reviews, and regulates project progress against baselines.
8. **Assess and Implement Changes** (§2.1.6.8) — Reviews and decides on change requests, evaluating their impact on value.
9. **Close Project or Phase** (§2.1.6.9) — Finalizes all activities to formally archive data, release resources, and transition benefits.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Governance acts as the integrative system that connects all other domains, ensuring cascading impacts are evaluated systemically (§2.1, §3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Governance frameworks protect and maximize value realization, ensuring project decisions are guided by the business case (§2.1.1, §3.4.3). |
| P8-04 — Accountable Leader | Governance formalizes authority boundaries, decision rights, and accountability escalation paths (§2.1.2, §3.6.3). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A01 — Business Case | The primary strategic justification document detailing value proposition and success criteria. |
| A04 — Project Charter | Authorizes the project manager and defines decision rights and governance boundaries. |
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | Integrates all domain plans and baselines into a single, cohesive operating system. |
| A35 — Governance and Decision Authority Record | Documents the steering committee charters, decision logs, and escalation paths. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-01-01 | Establish Governance Framework | Sets the boundary for decision rights, authority limits, and escalation rules. |
| SKL-02-01 | Develop Project Charter | Formulates and obtains approval for the formal chartering of the project or phase. |
| SKL-05-02 | Manage and Control Changes | Operationalizes the change request, impact assessment, and CCB approval processes. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Is our governance model "right-sized" to support value delivery without creating unnecessary bureaucracy?
2. Are project decisions and baselines regularly checked against the strategic objectives in the Business Case?
3. Do all team members and stakeholders know the correct escalation path for critical risks or scope changes?
4. Are we capturing lessons learned and project knowledge continuously, or only at project closure?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.1 — Governance Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD8-02 — Scope Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.2 — Scope Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Scope Performance Domain** addresses the definition, management, and control of the project scope, which is essential as the value of a project derives from the outcome delivered in alignment with its scope (PMBOK8 §2.2). It focuses on eliciting requirements, defining scope boundaries, breaking down work structure, and validating deliverables to prevent scope creep and ensure stakeholder satisfaction.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.2, effective execution of the Scope Performance Domain results in:
- **A clear, shared understanding** of the project and product scope among stakeholders and team members.
- **Systematic identification and management** of requirements, ensuring stakeholder needs are met.
- **Well-defined scope boundaries** that prevent unauthorized changes and control scope creep.
- **A decomposition of work (scope structure)** that provides a clear basis for planning and execution.
- **Formal acceptance and validation** of completed deliverables by relevant stakeholders.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 6 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.2.2):
1. **Plan Scope Management** (§2.2.2.1) — Documents how the project and product scope will be defined, validated, and controlled.
2. **Elicit and Analyze Requirements** (§2.2.2.2) — Determines, documents, and manages stakeholder needs and requirements.
3. **Define Scope** (§2.2.2.3) — Develops a detailed description of the project and product.
4. **Develop Scope Structure** (§2.2.2.4) — Subdivides project deliverables and work into smaller, more manageable components (e.g., Work Breakdown Structure - WBS, or product backlog).
5. **Monitor and Control Scope** (§2.2.2.5) — Monitors the status of the project and product scope and manages changes to the scope baseline.
6. **Validate Scope** (§2.2.2.6) — Formalizes acceptance of the completed project deliverables.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Scope definition must consider product lifecycle context, integration points, and second-order impacts (§3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Scope should be defined to maximize value and outcomes rather than simply delivering a high volume of outputs (§2.2, §3.4.3). |
| P8-03 — Embed Quality | Quality criteria (DoD and acceptance criteria) are integral attributes of scope, preventing defects and rework (§3.5.3). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A08 — Scope and Requirements Backlog | Documents requirements, user stories, scope statement, and WBS. |
| A13 — Requirements Traceability Matrix | Traces requirements from their origin to deliverables and test cases. |
| A17 — Performance Reporting Record | Tracks scope progress, variances, and completion metrics. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-01 | Define Project Scope and WBS | Systematically defines scope boundaries and decomposes work into WBS or backlogs. |
| SKL-03-04 | Elicit and Manage Requirements | Facilitates stakeholder workshops to elicit, analyze, and document requirements. |
| SKL-05-03 | Validate and Control Scope | Manages deliverable reviews, stakeholder sign-offs, and changes to scope baselines. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Are the boundaries of what is in-scope and out-of-scope explicitly documented and agreed upon?
2. How are we ensuring that every requirements item directly supports a target outcome in the Business Case?
3. What is our definition of done (DoD) for deliverables, and is it consistently understood by the team?
4. Are completed deliverables being validated progressively with the customer to ensure early feedback?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.2 — Scope Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD8-03 — Schedule Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.3 — Schedule Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Schedule Performance Domain** addresses activities and processes required to oversee the project's timely completion (PMBOK8 §2.3). Project scheduling provides a plan representing how and when the project will deliver the products, services, and results defined in the project scope. The schedule serves as a tool for communication, managing stakeholder expectations, and providing a basis for performance reporting.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.3, effective execution of the Schedule Performance Domain results in:
- **Scheduling approaches** that are consistent with the development approach selected (predictive, adaptive, or hybrid).
- **A project life cycle** consisting of phases that connect the delivery of business and stakeholder value from the beginning to the end.
- **A holistic approach** to delivering project outcomes with no gaps or areas of misalignment.
- **Complete schedule documentation** that includes dependencies, durations, resource allocations, and milestones.
- **Timely identification** of potential delays and risks, enabling proactive corrective actions to keep the project on track.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 3 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.3.2):
1. **Plan Schedule Management** (§2.3.2.1) — Establishes the policies, procedures, and documentation for planning, developing, managing, executing, and controlling the project schedule.
2. **Develop Schedule** (§2.3.2.2) — Analyzes activity sequences, durations, resource requirements, and schedule constraints to create the project schedule model.
3. **Monitor and Control Schedule** (§2.3.2.3) — Monitors the status of the project schedule, tracks actual progress against the baseline, and manages changes to the schedule model.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Scheduling must account for resource availability, risk buffers, and cross-project/portfolio dependencies (§2.3, §3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Schedules should be structured to enable progressive value realization (e.g., release planning) and avoid delays that erode ROI (§3.4.3). |
| P8-03 — Embed Quality | Prioritizes complete and correct scheduling practices and integrates quality gate reviews as scheduled milestones (§3.5.3). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A15 — Schedule Baseline | The approved schedule model used as a benchmark to measure actual progress and schedule variance. |
| A11 — Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RACI) | Defines who is responsible for schedule activities, ensuring tasks have clear owners. |
| A17 — Performance Reporting Record | Tracks schedule variance (SV) and schedule performance index (SPI) to report performance. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-05 | Plan Schedule Management | Defines the scheduling tools, methods, and variance thresholds for the project. |
| SKL-03-06b | Develop Schedule | Performs activity sequencing, duration estimation, and critical path analysis to build the schedule. |
| SKL-05-04 | Monitor and Control Schedule | Tracks schedule status, manages variances, and implements corrective actions or schedule changes. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Is our scheduling approach aligned with the delivery cadence and development approach of our deliverables?
2. Have we built in sufficient buffers for known-unknown risks to protect our target completion dates?
3. How frequently are we reviewing the critical path to anticipate and prevent schedule slippages?
4. Are schedule commitments co-created with the team members who will actually execute the work?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.3 — Schedule Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*

# PD8-04 — Finance Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.4 — Finance Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Finance Performance Domain** addresses activities and processes associated with planning, estimating, budgeting, and controlling costs so that the project can be completed within the approved budget and maximize investment returns (PMBOK8 §2.4). It focuses on aligning financial allocations with project priorities, establishing cost baselines, tracking variances, and conducting forecasting.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.4, effective execution of the Finance Performance Domain results in:
- **Financial plans and budgets** that are fully aligned with the development approach and project deliverables.
- **Accurate cost estimation** that accounts for direct, indirect, capital, and operating expenses.
- **Project completeness** within or below the approved budget, minimizing unauthorized cost increases.
- **Financial visibility and transparency** achieved through regular monitoring, variance analysis, and forecasting.
- **Optimized return on investment** by tracking financial performance against the business case.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 4 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.4.2):
1. **Plan Financial Management** (§2.4.2.1) — Establishes the policies, procedures, and documentation for planning, managing, expending, and controlling project costs.
2. **Estimate Costs** (§2.4.2.2) — Develops an approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project activities.
3. **Develop Budget** (§2.4.2.3) — Aggregates the estimated costs of individual activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline.
4. **Monitor and Control Finances** (§2.4.2.4) — Monitors the status of the project to update project costs and manages changes to the cost baseline.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Finance interacts with scope, schedule, risk reserves, and long-term organizational strategy to prevent project failures (§3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Financial resources must be managed as investments, continuously checking ROI, NPV, and IRR against the business case (§2.4, §3.4.3). |
| P8-03 — Embed Quality | Embedding quality in financial processes prevents rework, scrap, and wasteful overhead expenditures (§3.5.3). |
| P8-05 — Integrate Sustainability | Finance includes lifecycle cost analysis, social return on investment (SROI), and environmental cost accounting (§3.7.3). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A16 — Financial Baseline | The approved budget version, excluding management reserves, used to measure cost variance. |
| A01 — Business Case | Defines target financial metrics (payback period, ROI, CapEx/OpEx boundaries). |
| A20 — Quantitative Risk Reserve Decision Record | Documents contingency and management reserve allocations based on risk exposure. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-07 | Plan Financial Management and Budgeting | Establishes budget models, control thresholds, and tracking mechanisms. |
| SKL-05-13 | Perform Earned Value Analysis | Calculates EVM metrics (CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC) and forecasts financial completion. |
| SKL-05-05 | Monitor and Control Finances | Manages invoices, tracks actual expenditure, and regulates cost baseline changes. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Are our cost estimates based on historical data, vendor quotes, or parametric modeling, and how high is their uncertainty?
2. Are we tracking actual expenditures progressively, or are we discovering cost overruns too late?
3. How are contingency and management reserves being managed, and what is the approval threshold for their release?
4. Does the team understand how scope or schedule delays affect our cash flow and overall financial viability?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.4 — Finance Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD8-05 — Stakeholders Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.5 — Stakeholders Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Stakeholders Performance Domain** addresses the processes, activities, and tools related to stakeholder engagement and communication throughout the project lifecycle (PMBOK8 §2.5). It covers identifying stakeholders, understanding their interest and influence, planning and managing their engagement, and managing and monitoring communications to ensure stakeholder alignment and satisfaction.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.5, effective execution of the Stakeholders Performance Domain results in:
- **A productive working relationship** with stakeholders, fostering trust and collaboration.
- **Stakeholder agreement and alignment** with project objectives and definition of value.
- **Satisfied project beneficiaries** who find the deliverables responsive to their needs.
- **Proactive identification and management** of conflicting stakeholder interests.
- **Effective information flow** ensuring that the right message reaches the right stakeholder at the right time.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 7 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.5.2):
1. **Identify Stakeholders** (§2.5.2.1) — Identifies all people, groups, or organizations affected by the project, analyzing their interest and influence.
2. **Plan Stakeholder Engagement** (§2.5.2.2) — Develops approaches to involve project stakeholders based on their needs, expectations, and interest.
3. **Plan Communications Management** (§2.5.2.3) — Develops an appropriate approach for project communication activities based on information needs.
4. **Manage Stakeholder Engagement** (§2.5.2.4) — Communicates and works with stakeholders to meet their needs, address issues, and foster engagement.
5. **Manage Communications** (§2.5.2.5) — Creation, collection, distribution, storage, retrieval, and ultimate disposition of project information.
6. **Monitor Stakeholder Engagement** (§2.5.2.6) — Monitors stakeholder relationships and tailors strategies for engaging stakeholders.
7. **Monitor Communications** (§2.5.2.7) — Ensures the information needs of the project and its stakeholders are met.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Fosters collaboration by integrating diverse stakeholder perspectives across the entire project ecosystem (§3.3.1, §3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Stakeholder expectations define what value is; engagement ensures outcomes remain aligned with these definitions (§3.4.3). |
| P8-06 — Build an Empowered Culture | Motivated and empowered teams actively engage with stakeholders to maximize value delivery and transparency (§3.8.3, Figure 3-9). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | Documents all identified stakeholders, roles, interest, power, and contact info. |
| A28 — Communication and Engagement Record | Logs communications distributed, meetings conducted, and feedback gathered. |
| A29 — Change Readiness and Adoption Record | Evaluates stakeholder readiness and adoption curves for organizational changes. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-02 | Identify Stakeholders | Systematically discovers and registers all project stakeholders and attributes. |
| SKL-04-06 | Manage Stakeholder Engagement | Conducts workshops, facilitates discussions, and resolves stakeholder conflicts. |
| SKL-05-07 | Monitor Stakeholder Engagement | Tracks sentiment, updates the stakeholder register, and adjusts engagement plans. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Have we identified all key stakeholder groups, including those who are silent or indirectly affected?
2. Are our communications tailored to the preferences and language needs of different stakeholder groups?
3. How are we capturing and addressing stakeholder resistance or negative feedback early?
4. Are we verifying that project information reaches the target audience, or are we just sending it out?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.5 — Stakeholders Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD8-06 — Resources Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.6 — Resources Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Resources Performance Domain** addresses how effectively and efficiently a project team plans for and utilizes its available resources (PMBOK8 §2.6). This domain encompasses both human resources (the project team itself) and physical, virtual, or digital resources (equipment, materials, software, facilities, infrastructure, licenses, services, information, or documents), covering their availability, utilization, and maintenance.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.6, effective execution of the Resources Performance Domain results in:
- **Shared ownership of the project** — the project team understands the vision, objectives, and owns deliverables.
- **A high-performing project team** — built on mutual trust, collaboration, and resilience.
- **Effective resource utilization** — actual resource usage versus planned is actively managed.
- **Acceptable downtime percentage** for physical, virtual, or digital resources.
- **Productive resource management** — minimizing material waste, scrap, and unnecessary resource conflicts.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 5 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.6.2):
1. **Plan Resource Management** (§2.6.2.1) — Defines how to estimate, acquire, manage, and utilize physical and team resources.
2. **Estimate Resources** (§2.6.2.2) — Estimates team resources and the type and quantities of materials, equipment, or software.
3. **Acquire Resources** (§2.6.2.3) — Obtains team members, facilities, equipment, materials, software, and other resources.
4. **Lead the Team** (§2.6.2.4) — Directs and coaches the team, fostering motivation, skills, and professional and ethical behaviors.
5. **Monitor and Control Resourcing** (§2.6.2.5) — Ensures that assigned resources are available and utilized as planned.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Resources must be managed contextually, recognizing skill gaps, allocation overlaps, and energy/tool footprints (§3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Right resource at the right time at the right cost optimizes value and minimizes wasted budget (§3.4.3). |
| P8-03 — Embed Quality | Assigns skilled personnel and appropriate tools to maintain target quality thresholds and prevent defect creation (§3.5.3). |
| P8-06 — Empowered Culture | Team members are empowered, motivated, and supported, enabling collaborative problem-solving and self-organization (§3.8.3). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A10 — Team Charter | Co-creates team working agreements, values, norms, and collaboration rules. |
| A11 — Responsibility Assignment Matrix (RACI) | Assigns roles and responsibilities, mapping activities to team members. |
| A25 — Team Operating Record | Tracks team composition, skills, training, satisfaction, and availability. |
| A31 — Procurement and Supplier Management Record | Tracks physical resource procurement, supplier performance, and deliveries. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-12 | Plan Resource Management | Establishes the resource management plan, team structures, and procurement paths. |
| SKL-03-13 | Define Team Charter and Agreements | Establishes the team's behavioral norms and working agreements. |
| SKL-04-07 | Manage and Lead Project Teams | Motivates the team, builds trust, manages conflict, and conducts evaluations. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Do we have the right mix of skills and experience on the team to achieve our project objectives?
2. Are physical, virtual, or digital resources (e.g., test environments, software licenses) available when needed?
3. How are we monitoring resource downtime and managing conflicts over shared resources?
4. Are team members experiencing burnout, and how are we supporting their growth and satisfaction?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.6 — Resources Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD8-07 — Risk Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.7 — Risk Performance Domain  
**Version:** 1.0.0  

## Definition
The **Risk Performance Domain** addresses activities and processes associated with risk and uncertainty (including ambiguity, complexity, and volatility) through systematic identification, analysis, and response across the project lifecycle (PMBOK8 §2.7). It focuses on minimizing threats to project objectives and exploiting opportunities to enhance project value.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.7, effective execution of the Risk Performance Domain results in:
- **Systematic identification** of project risks (both threats and opportunities) throughout the lifecycle.
- **Qualitative and quantitative analysis** of risks, understanding their probability, impact, and urgency.
- **Pragmatic risk response strategies** that are cost-effective, agreed-upon, and owned.
- **Proactive implementation** of risk responses, minimizing negative consequences and capturing benefits.
- **Continuous monitoring of the risk landscape**, adapting risk registers and reserve levels to changing conditions.

## Key Processes and Activities
This domain encompasses the following 6 canonical processes (PMBOK8 §2.7.2):
1. **Plan Risk Management** (§2.7.2.1) — Defines how to conduct risk management activities for a project.
2. **Identify Risks** (§2.7.2.2) — Identifies individual project risks as well as sources of overall project risk, documenting their characteristics.
3. **Perform Risk Analysis** (§2.7.2.3) — Performs qualitative risk analysis (prioritizing risks by probability/impact) and/or quantitative risk analysis (modeling overall project risk exposure).
4. **Plan Risk Responses** (§2.7.2.4) — Develops options, selects strategies, and agrees on actions to address overall project risk exposure and treat individual risks.
5. **Implement Risk Responses** (§2.7.2.5) — Implements agreed-upon risk response plans.
6. **Monitor Risks** (§2.7.2.6) — Monitors implementation of risk response plans, tracks identified risks, identifies and analyzes new risks, and evaluates risk process effectiveness.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P8-01 — Holistic View | Risk exists across all domains; holistic thinking prevents blind spots and addresses cross-domain cascading risks (§3.3.3). |
| P8-02 — Focus on Value | Risk management protects value and targets opportunities that can enhance strategic return on investment (§3.4.3). |
| P8-03 — Embed Quality | Encompasses the proactive management of quality-related risks, preventing defects and compliance failures (§3.5.3). |
| P8-05 — Integrate Sustainability | Sustainability risks (social, regulatory, environmental, reputational) require active identification and management (§3.7.1). |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A19 — Risk Management Record (Risk Register) | The central database documenting identified risks, owners, analysis, and response plans. |
| A20 — Quantitative Risk Reserve Decision Record | Documents the contingency reserve allocations based on quantitative simulations. |
| A18 — Issue, Impediment, and Action Log | Tracks realized risks (issues) and details immediate resolution actions. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-03-14 | Plan and Manage Project Risks | Establishes the risk approach, leads risk workshops, and defines registers. |
| SKL-03-15 | Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis | Conducts simulations (e.g., Monte Carlo) to estimate budget/schedule reserves. |
| SKL-05-08 | Monitor and Control Project Risks | Regularly updates the risk register, tracks metrics, and manages triggers. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Are we identifying opportunities (positive risks) as actively as we are identifying threats (negative risks)?
2. Do we have clearly assigned owners for every key risk, and are they authorized to implement responses?
3. How are we checking if our risk responses are actually effective, or are we just checking off activities?
4. Are overall project risks (e.g., environmental, political, or economic volatility) being evaluated alongside task-level risks?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.7 — Risk Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# Performance Domains Index — PMBOK 8 Canonical
**Type:** Reference Index  
**Edition:** PMBOK 8th Edition (2025)  
**PMBOK 8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide Part 2 §2.1–§2.7 — Performance Domains  
**Version:** 2.0.0  

## Purpose
Performance domains are the **seven** interactive, interrelated, and interdependent areas of focus that work in unison to achieve the desired project results (Guide §2). PMBOK 8 reorganizes the Guide around these seven domains, replacing the ten Knowledge Areas from PMBOK 6 and the eight domains from PMBOK 7.

Performance domains are not sequential phases; they represent concurrent areas of focus that operate simultaneously from initiation through closure.

## The 7 PMBOK 8 Performance Domains

| ID | Domain Name | Guide §Ref | Brief Description | Reference File |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PD8-01 | Governance | §2.1 | Framework, functions, and processes that guide project decisions and activities to optimize value delivery. Includes integration, strategic alignment, and change control. | [PD8-01-governance.md](PD8-01-governance.md) |
| PD8-02 | Scope | §2.2 | Definition, management, and control of project boundaries, requirements elicitation, work breakdown structure, and formal acceptance. | [PD8-02-scope.md](PD8-02-scope.md) |
| PD8-03 | Schedule | §2.3 | Activity sequencing, duration estimation, schedule model development, critical path analysis, and variance monitoring. | [PD8-03-schedule.md](PD8-03-schedule.md) |
| PD8-04 | Finance | §2.4 | Cost estimation, budgeting, cash flow tracking, forecasting, and earned value analysis (EVM). | [PD8-04-finance.md](PD8-04-finance.md) |
| PD8-05 | Stakeholders | §2.5 | Stakeholder identification, analysis, engagement planning, communications management, and relationship monitoring. | [PD8-05-stakeholders.md](PD8-05-stakeholders.md) |
| PD8-06 | Resources | §2.6 | Acquisition, management, motivation, and development of the team, and efficient utilization of physical, virtual, or digital assets. | [PD8-06-resources.md](PD8-06-resources.md) |
| PD8-07 | Risk | §2.7 | Identification, qualitative/quantitative analysis, response planning, and implementation of treatments for project threats and opportunities. | [PD8-07-risk.md](PD8-07-risk.md) |

---

## Domain Interaction and Integration
- **Governance** serves as the central coordinating mechanism that monitors scope, schedule, and financial trade-offs to maximize value delivery.
- **Scope, Schedule, and Finance** are the core delivery baselines, where changes in one systemically impact the others.
- **Stakeholders and Resources** drive execution; human interactions, skills, and effective communication enable WBS tasks.
- **Risk** spans all domains, identifying threats to baselines and opportunities to optimize resources or schedule speed.

---

## PMBOK 7 Heritage Reference (Cross-Edition Traceability)
> PMBOK 7 utilized eight performance domains (Stakeholders, Team, Dev Approach, Planning, Project Work, Delivery, Measurement, Uncertainty). PMBOK 8 synthesizes and restructures these into its seven canonical domains. Historical PMBOK 7 files have been moved to the `pmbok7-archive/` directory.

| PMBOK 7 Performance Domain | Primary Destination in PMBOK 8 |
|---|---|
| Stakeholders | PD8-05 — Stakeholders Performance Domain |
| Team | PD8-06 — Resources Performance Domain (Lead the Team) |
| Development Approach and Life Cycle | PD8-01 — Governance / PD8-02 — Scope / PD8-03 — Schedule |
| Planning | Fully integrated across all domains (PD8-01 through PD8-07) |
| Project Work | PD8-01 — Governance / PD8-06 — Resources / PD8-05 — Stakeholders |
| Delivery | PD8-02 — Scope Performance Domain (including quality) |
| Measurement | PD8-01 — Governance (Monitor and Control Project Performance) / EVM |
| Uncertainty | PD8-07 — Risk Performance Domain |

---

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide Part 2 §2.1–§2.7 · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD01 — Stakeholders Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain
**PMBOK8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.1 — Stakeholders Performance Domain
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
The Stakeholders Performance Domain addresses the activities and functions associated with stakeholders throughout the project life cycle (PMBOK8 §2.1). A stakeholder is any individual, group, or organization that may affect, be affected by, or perceive itself to be affected by a decision, activity, or outcome of a project. This domain focuses on identifying who stakeholders are, understanding their interests, needs, expectations, and degree of influence, and then working continuously to build productive working relationships, manage engagement, resolve conflicts of interest, and align stakeholder expectations with project objectives. The domain recognizes that stakeholder relationships are dynamic — they evolve as the project progresses — and therefore require ongoing attention rather than a single up-front analysis.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.1, effective execution of the Stakeholders Performance Domain results in the following outcomes:

- **A productive working relationship with stakeholders throughout the project** — stakeholders are engaged, informed, and collaborative rather than passive or adversarial.
- **Stakeholder agreement with project objectives** — expectations are aligned early and continuously revalidated so that scope, schedule, and value definitions reflect shared understanding.
- **Stakeholders who are project beneficiaries are satisfied** — those who receive the project's outputs or are affected by its outcomes experience the project as responsive to their needs.
- **Stakeholder resistance to change is minimized** — proactive engagement reduces the friction that organizational or personal resistance can create for project delivery.
- **Conflicting stakeholder interests are identified and managed** — tensions between stakeholder groups are surfaced, addressed, and resolved rather than suppressed or ignored.

## Key Activities and Considerations
Grounded in PMBOK8 §2.1, practitioners operating in this domain carry out the following:

- **Identify stakeholders continuously** — Initial identification occurs early, but the stakeholder landscape changes as the project evolves; new stakeholders emerge and existing ones shift in influence or interest.
- **Analyze stakeholder characteristics** — Understand each stakeholder's interests, expectations, role, degree of influence, and potential impact on and from the project to prioritize engagement approaches.
- **Plan and execute tailored engagement** — Develop and implement engagement strategies appropriate to each stakeholder's profile; one-size-fits-all communication is insufficient for complex stakeholder landscapes.
- **Build trust through consistent communication** — Regular, transparent, and honest communication establishes credibility and reduces the likelihood of surprise or opposition.
- **Monitor stakeholder engagement levels** — Track whether engagement activities are achieving the desired results and adapt strategies when stakeholders become disengaged, resistant, or misaligned.
- **Manage conflicting interests** — When stakeholder groups hold competing priorities or expectations, surface these conflicts explicitly and work with governance structures and the team to resolve or balance them.
- **Close out stakeholder relationships at project completion** — Formally recognize stakeholder contributions, communicate final outcomes, and conduct lessons learned activities that include stakeholder feedback.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P03 — Stakeholders | This principle directly grounds the domain: project teams engage stakeholders proactively, understand their needs and expectations, and work to foster positive relationships. The domain operationalizes this principle across the project life cycle. |
| P06 — Leadership | Effective stakeholder engagement requires leadership behaviors — empathy, active listening, conflict resolution, and the ability to build coalitions. Project managers and team leaders apply leadership skills to navigate complex stakeholder dynamics. |
| P12 — Change | Stakeholder engagement is inseparable from change management; stakeholders are the people who must accept, adopt, or adapt to project-driven change. Understanding readiness and resistance is central to both this principle and this domain. |
| P04 — Value | Stakeholder perspectives define what constitutes value; understanding and aligning with those perspectives ensures the project delivers outcomes that are genuinely beneficial rather than technically correct but organizationally rejected. |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | The primary artifact of this domain. Documents all identified stakeholders, their roles, interests, influence levels, engagement status, and communication preferences. It is created early and updated throughout the project life cycle. |
| A10 — Communications Management Plan | Defines how, when, and in what format information is communicated to each stakeholder group. Directly supports planned and targeted engagement. |
| A28 — Communications Reporting Record | Documents the actual communication activities carried out — meeting records, status reports distributed, and engagement interactions logged. Provides an audit trail of stakeholder engagement execution. |
| A29 — Change Readiness and Adoption Record | Tracks stakeholder readiness for project-driven organizational change. Links stakeholder engagement to change management outcomes, especially for projects with significant organizational impact. |
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | Incorporates the stakeholder engagement approach and communications strategy as integrated components; stakeholder decisions influence scope, schedule, and delivery planning. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-02-02 | Identify Stakeholders | The foundational skill for this domain — systematically identifies all parties who may affect or be affected by the project, analyzes their characteristics, and creates the Stakeholder Register. |
| SKL-04-06 | Manage Stakeholder Engagement | The execution skill — implements the engagement strategy, builds relationships, addresses concerns, resolves issues, and fosters stakeholder participation throughout the project. |
| SKL-05-07 | Monitor Stakeholder Engagement | The control skill — tracks whether engagement efforts are achieving desired outcomes, identifies shifts in stakeholder posture, and triggers adjustments to engagement strategies as needed. |

## Interaction with Other Domains
| Domain | Interaction |
|---|---|
| PD02 — Team | Team members are also stakeholders; the Stakeholders domain includes the project team in its analysis, while the Team domain focuses specifically on enabling the people doing the work. Engagement principles apply internally as well as externally. |
| PD06 — Delivery | Stakeholders are the ultimate recipients and evaluators of deliverables. Their acceptance criteria, satisfaction signals, and feedback loops connect delivery outcomes directly to stakeholder engagement health. |
| PD07 — Measurement | Stakeholder satisfaction is a key project outcome metric. Measurement activities include assessing engagement levels, tracking sentiment, and evaluating whether stakeholder expectations are being met over time. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Have all affected stakeholder groups been identified, including those who may be indirectly impacted or who perceive themselves to be affected?
2. Do we understand each key stakeholder's real interests and motivations — not just their stated positions — and are we engaging accordingly?
3. Are there unresolved conflicts between stakeholder groups that could derail acceptance, adoption, or delivery of project outcomes?
4. Is our stakeholder engagement approach being adjusted as the project evolves, or are we relying on an outdated initial analysis?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.1 — Stakeholders Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*

# PD02 — Team Performance Domain
**Type:** Performance Domain
**PMBOK8 Source:** PMBOK8 Guide §2.2 — Team Performance Domain
**Version:** 1.0.0

## Definition
The Team Performance Domain addresses the activities and functions associated with the people responsible for producing project outcomes (PMBOK8 §2.2). This domain covers team composition, roles and responsibilities, team development, leadership styles, performance management, conflict resolution, and the creation of conditions that enable high performance. Critically, the domain encompasses both the project manager's or leader's role in enabling the team AND the contributions of individual team members — it is not solely about managing people from a position of authority, but about cultivating a collaborative environment in which the team as a whole can deliver. PMBOK8 §2.2 reflects a values-based approach to team performance, emphasizing psychological safety, shared accountability, and servant leadership alongside structural clarity.

## Desired Outcomes
Per PMBOK8 §2.2, effective execution of the Team Performance Domain results in the following outcomes:

- **Shared ownership of project outcomes** — team members understand their collective responsibility for project success, not merely their individual task assignments.
- **A high-performing team** — the team demonstrates collaboration, trust, adaptability, and consistent delivery of quality work.
- **Leadership behaviors demonstrated by all team members** — leadership is not confined to the project manager; team members at all levels contribute initiative, problem-solving, and accountability.
- **Appropriate project leadership style applied** — the project manager or leader adapts their style to the team's development stage, the project context, and individual team member needs.
- **Team members have the skills, competencies, and experience necessary** — gaps are identified and addressed through development, coaching, or resource adjustment so the team is equipped for what the project requires.

## Key Activities and Considerations
Grounded in PMBOK8 §2.2, practitioners operating in this domain carry out the following:

- **Define team composition and roles** — Identify the skills, expertise, and capacity required for the project; establish clear roles and responsibilities so team members understand what is expected of them and of each other.
- **Develop the team** — Invest in building team cohesion, improving collaboration, and developing individual and collective competencies through training, coaching, mentoring, and structured team-building activities.
- **Apply appropriate leadership styles** — Recognize that no single leadership approach fits all situations; adapt between directive, facilitative, coaching, and servant leadership styles depending on team maturity and project context.
- **Manage team performance** — Monitor individual and team performance against expectations, recognize contributions, address performance gaps respectfully and constructively, and create feedback loops that support continuous improvement.
- **Resolve conflict** — Address interpersonal and role-based conflicts promptly using principled negotiation, mediation, or escalation as appropriate; unresolved conflict degrades team performance and project outcomes.
- **Foster psychological safety and inclusion** — Create an environment where team members feel safe to raise concerns, challenge assumptions, and contribute ideas without fear of negative consequences.
- **Manage distributed and virtual teams** — Apply additional deliberate effort to maintain communication, cohesion, and accountability in teams that are geographically dispersed or operate asynchronously.

## Relationship to Principles
| Principle | Connection |
|---|---|
| P02 — Team | This principle directly grounds the domain: project teams are composed of individuals who collectively deliver project outcomes. The domain operationalizes this principle by defining how teams are built, led, developed, and sustained. |
| P06 — Leadership | Leadership is central to the Team domain — not as a positional authority function but as a behavioral quality that the project manager models and that all team members are encouraged to demonstrate in their areas of contribution. |
| P03 — Stakeholders | Team members are internal stakeholders with their own needs, expectations, and engagement requirements. The Stakeholders principle reminds practitioners that the team itself must be engaged, not merely directed. |
| P10 — Quality | Team capability and collaboration directly influence quality outcomes. A high-performing team with clear roles, strong feedback practices, and a shared commitment to excellence is the foundation for producing quality deliverables. |

## Artifact Connections
| Artifact | Connection |
|---|---|
| A25 — Team Operating and Responsibility Record | The primary artifact of this domain. Documents team roles, responsibilities, operating agreements, decision rights, and behavioral norms. Establishes shared expectations and accountability structures within the team. |
| A26 — Resource Capacity and Acquisition Record | Tracks team resource availability, capacity constraints, and acquisition decisions. Informs staffing, onboarding, and the management of resource gaps or surpluses across the project. |
| A07 — Stakeholder Register | Team members are identified and documented as internal stakeholders in the Stakeholder Register. Engagement characteristics, interests, and communication preferences for team members are captured here alongside external stakeholders. |
| A14 — Integrated Project Management Plan | Incorporates the resource management plan and team development approach. The Team domain's decisions about composition, roles, and leadership style are reflected in the integrated plan and shape execution. |

## Skill Connections
| Skill ID | Skill Name | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| SKL-04-04 | Develop Team | The core developmental skill for this domain — builds team cohesion, competencies, and collaborative capability through structured activities, coaching, feedback, and learning opportunities. |
| SKL-04-05 | Manage Team | The operational performance skill — tracks team performance, addresses conflicts and issues, recognizes contributions, and adjusts team structures or assignments to maintain delivery capability. |
| SKL-03-12 | Plan Resource Management | The planning skill — determines how team resources will be identified, acquired, developed, managed, and released. Establishes the resource management framework within which the team operates. |

## Interaction with Other Domains
| Domain | Interaction |
|---|---|
| PD01 — Stakeholders | Team members are internal stakeholders; engagement, communication, and expectation alignment principles from the Stakeholders domain apply to the team. Conversely, how the team operates directly affects stakeholder relationships and project credibility. |
| PD05 — Project Work | The team is the primary agent of project work execution. Team capability, collaboration quality, and performance management directly determine how effectively project work is planned, coordinated, and delivered. |
| PD07 — Measurement | Team performance is a key measurement focus — velocity, quality metrics, schedule adherence, and issue resolution rates all reflect team health. Measurement results feed back into team development and leadership decisions. |

## Questions to Ask in Practice
1. Does every team member clearly understand their role, their accountability, and how their work connects to the project's objectives and value delivery?
2. What is the current team development stage, and is the leadership approach being applied appropriate to that stage?
3. Are there unresolved conflicts, capability gaps, or engagement issues within the team that are affecting performance or creating delivery risk?
4. Is the team environment psychologically safe — do team members feel empowered to raise concerns, propose alternatives, and hold each other accountable?

*Authority: PMBOK8 Guide §2.2 — Team Performance Domain · Source type: PMI-derived*
*Repository: https://github.com/fakhruldeen/PMOSkills*
